questions.question: What is the process of investigating computer equipment and associated storage media to determine if it has been used in the commission of a crime or for unauthorized activities?
options: Computer Forensics
Criminal Forensics
Criminal Enquiry
Computer Emergency
questions.explanation: Computer Forensics is the discipline of scientifically investigating, collecting, preserving, and analyzing digital evidence from computer systems and storage media to determine if they have been used in criminal or unauthorized activities. It follows strict legal procedures to ensure evidence admissibility.
questions.takeaway: Computer Forensics = Investigating digital devices for evidence of criminal/unauthorized activity

questions.question: When a file is deleted on a FAT-based system, what actually happens?
options: All of the above.
The first character of the directory entry file name is changed to a special character.
The FAT entry for the file is zeroed out so it shows that the area is available for use by a new file.
The file remains intact.
questions.explanation: When a file is deleted on a FAT file system, the operating system changes the first character of the directory entry filename to a special character (0xE5 in hexadecimal). The FAT entries are marked as available but data is NOT immediately erased, which is why deleted files can be recovered forensically.
questions.takeaway: File deletion on FAT = First directory entry character replaced with 0xE5; data still present until overwritten

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a property of computer evidence?
options: Complete and Convincing.
Duplicated and Preserved.
Conform and Human Readable.
Authentic and Accurate.
questions.explanation: Properties of valid computer evidence include: Authentic and Accurate (genuinely from the scene), Complete and Convincing (tells the full story), and Duplicated and Preserved (bit-stream copy verified). 'Conform and Human Readable' is not a standard recognized property of computer evidence.
questions.takeaway: Computer evidence properties: Authentic, Accurate, Complete, Convincing, Duplicated, Preserved — NOT 'Conform and Human Readable'

questions.question: You can use ________, a powerful search tool, to perform keyword searches in Linux and in EnCase software.
options: gcc
gnu
grub
grep
questions.explanation: grep (Global Regular Expression Print) is a powerful command-line search utility in Linux used to search for patterns within files. EnCase, a popular forensic tool, also supports grep-style searching for keyword searches across evidence files.
questions.takeaway: grep = Powerful keyword search tool used in Linux and EnCase forensic software

questions.question: You are a computer forensic examiner at a scene about to seize a Linux server containing fraud-related database records. After photographing the screen and noting running programs, what should you do next?
options: Pull the plug from the rear of the computer
Pull the plug from the wall
Use the normal shutdown procedure
Ask the user at the scene to shut down the server
questions.explanation: Best forensic practice when seizing a running Linux server is to pull the power cord from the rear of the computer. This avoids running shutdown scripts that could alter or destroy evidence, unlike a normal shutdown. Pulling from the wall is less preferred because surge protectors/UPS may be involved. Never let a suspect shut down.
questions.takeaway: Linux server seizure = Pull plug from rear of computer to avoid evidence-destroying shutdown scripts

questions.question: When a forensic copy is made, in what format are the contents of the hard drive stored?
options: As bootable files.
As compressed images.
As executable files.
As operating system files.
questions.explanation: A forensic copy (forensic image) stores the contents of a hard drive as a compressed image (e.g., E01/DD format). This bit-stream copy captures every sector including deleted files, unallocated space, and slack space, creating an exact replica for analysis without altering the original.
questions.takeaway: Forensic copy = Compressed image format capturing every bit of the original drive

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a type of volatile evidence?
options: Main Memory
Cached Data
Log files
Routing Tables
questions.explanation: Volatile evidence is data that is lost when a system is powered off: RAM/main memory, routing tables (maintained in memory), cached data, and running processes. Log files are stored on disk (non-volatile storage) and persist after shutdown, making them non-volatile evidence.
questions.takeaway: Volatile evidence = RAM, routing tables, cache, running processes — Log files are NON-volatile (stored on disk)

questions.question: In establishing what evidence is admissible, many rules of evidence concentrate first on the _____________ of the offered evidence.
options: Material
Admissibility
Search and Seizure
Relevancy
questions.explanation: Relevancy is the first and foundational criterion for admissibility of evidence. Evidence must be relevant — meaning it tends to make a fact of consequence more or less probable — before other criteria like materiality and authenticity are evaluated.
questions.takeaway: Evidence admissibility: Relevancy is the first criterion evaluated before materiality and authenticity

questions.question: Which of the following is a proper acquisition technique?
options: All of the above
Disk to Disk
Sparse Acquisition
Disk to Image
questions.explanation: All three are valid forensic acquisition techniques: Disk-to-Image creates a bit-for-bit image file (E01/DD); Disk-to-Disk creates an exact physical copy on another drive; Sparse Acquisition captures only allocated data and is used for very large storage arrays where full imaging is impractical.
questions.takeaway: Forensic acquisition methods: Disk-to-Image, Disk-to-Disk, and Sparse Acquisition are all valid techniques

questions.question: Traditional crimes that became easier or more widespread because of telecommunication networks and powerful PCs include all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Illegal drug distribution
DoS attacks
Money laundering
Child pornography
questions.explanation: DoS (Denial of Service) attacks are a new category of crime created entirely by computer technology — they did not exist before computers/networks. Money laundering, illegal drug distribution, and child pornography are traditional crimes that technology made easier or more widespread, but DoS attacks are purely cyber-native crimes.
questions.takeaway: DoS attacks = New cyber-native crime; Money laundering, drug distribution, child pornography = Traditional crimes aided by technology

questions.question: _____________ devices prevent altering data on drives attached to the suspect computer and also offer very fast acquisition speeds.
options: Encryption
Imaging
Write Blocking
Hashing
questions.explanation: Write blockers (hardware or software) are essential forensic tools that prevent any write operations to the suspect drive while allowing read operations. They ensure evidence integrity by preventing accidental modification during acquisition. Hardware write blockers also offer high-speed data transfer.
questions.takeaway: Write Blockers = Forensic devices preventing drive modification during evidence acquisition

questions.question: Which duplication method produces an exact replica of the original drive?
options: Drive Image
Mirror Copy
Image Copy
Bit-Stream Copy
questions.explanation: A Bit-Stream Copy (also called a physical copy or sector-by-sector copy) duplicates every single bit from the source drive including unallocated space, deleted files, and file slack. It creates an exact sector-level replica that is forensically verified using hash values (MD5/SHA1).
questions.takeaway: Bit-Stream Copy = Sector-by-sector exact replica of drive including unallocated space and deleted data

questions.question: To verify the original drive with the forensic copy, you use __________.
options: a hash analysis
a password
disk to disk verification
none of the above
questions.explanation: Hash analysis (using MD5 or SHA-1/SHA-256) creates a unique digital fingerprint of the original drive. After creating a forensic copy, the investigator hashes both the original and the copy. Matching hash values prove the copy is forensically sound and unaltered.
questions.takeaway: Forensic copy verification = Hash analysis (MD5/SHA-1) — matching hashes confirm bit-perfect copy

questions.question: The Windows operating system uses a file name's ___________ to associate files with the proper applications.
options: MD5 hash value
Extension
Metadata
Signature
questions.explanation: Windows uses file extensions (e.g., .docx, .pdf, .exe) to associate files with applications through the registry. This is forensically important because attackers may change extensions to disguise files — forensic tools compare file headers (magic numbers) against extensions to detect mismatches.
questions.takeaway: Windows file association = File extension; Forensic tip: Compare extension vs file header to detect disguised files

questions.question: As a good forensic practice, why would it be a good idea to wipe a forensic drive before using it?
options: Chain of Custody
No need to wipe
Cross-contamination
Different file and operating systems
questions.explanation: Wiping (degaussing or overwriting with zeros) a forensic drive before use prevents cross-contamination — residual data from a previous investigation could appear in unallocated space on the target drive and be mistakenly attributed to the current case. This maintains evidence integrity.
questions.takeaway: Pre-use drive wiping = Prevents cross-contamination of evidence from previous investigations

questions.question: The ability to hide data in another file is called:
options: Encryption.
A and B.
Data parsing.
Steganography.
questions.explanation: Steganography is the practice of hiding secret data within an ordinary, non-secret file or message to avoid detection. Unlike encryption (which makes data unreadable), steganography conceals the existence of the data itself — hiding it inside images, audio files, or documents.
questions.takeaway: Steganography = Hiding data inside another file; Encryption = Making data unreadable (different concepts)

questions.question: When two hard drives are on the same data cable, both drives must have which two settings for them to work?
options: Master and Slave
Primary and Secondary
First and Second
Default and Cable Select
questions.explanation: On IDE/PATA interfaces, when two drives share the same cable, one must be configured as Master and the other as Slave using jumper settings. This is relevant in forensics when investigators encounter older computer setups during evidence collection.
questions.takeaway: IDE dual-drive configuration = Master and Slave jumper settings on the same cable

questions.question: USB drives use ______________.
options: None of the above
Flash memory
RAM memory
Cache memory
questions.explanation: USB drives (flash drives/thumb drives) use NAND flash memory — a non-volatile storage technology that retains data without power. Flash memory is electrically erasable and reprogrammable, making it ideal for portable storage. Forensically, deleted data may be recoverable from flash memory.
questions.takeaway: USB drives = Flash memory (non-volatile NAND); data survives power-off and may be forensically recoverable

questions.question: Which of the following is a proper search technique in computer forensics?
options: Regular Expression Search
Keyword Search
All of the above
Manual Browsing
questions.explanation: All three are valid forensic search techniques: Manual Browsing (visually inspecting files), Keyword Search (searching for specific terms), and Regular Expression Search (pattern-matching using regex). Forensic tools like EnCase and FTK support all three methods for comprehensive evidence discovery.
questions.takeaway: Forensic search techniques: Manual Browsing + Keyword Search + Regular Expression Search — all are valid

questions.question: A file header is which of the following?
options: Synonymous with the file extension
A 128-bit value that is unique to a specific file based on its data
A unique set of characters following the file name that identifies the file type
A unique set of characters at the beginning of a file that identifies the file type
questions.explanation: A file header (also called a magic number or file signature) consists of specific bytes at the very beginning of a file that identify the file type. For example, JPEG files start with 'FF D8 FF', PDF files start with '%PDF'. Forensic tools use file headers to identify file types, even when extensions have been changed.
questions.takeaway: File header = Magic bytes at file start identifying true file type; Used forensically to detect renamed/disguised files

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a true operating system?
options: Windows 3.1
DOS
UNIX
Windows 2000
questions.explanation: Windows 3.1 was a graphical user interface (GUI) shell that ran on top of MS-DOS, not a true standalone operating system. It lacked its own kernel and relied on DOS for basic OS services. In contrast, DOS, Windows 2000, and UNIX are complete operating systems with their own kernels.
questions.takeaway: Windows 3.1 = GUI shell over DOS, NOT a true OS; Windows 2000, DOS, UNIX = True operating systems

questions.question: Computer memory files written to the hard drive are called ____________.
options: User profiles
Swap files
Spool files
Metadata
questions.explanation: Swap files (also called page files or virtual memory files — pagefile.sys in Windows) are disk-based extensions of RAM. When physical memory is exhausted, the OS writes memory contents to disk as swap/page files. These are forensically valuable as they may contain passwords, encryption keys, or browser history.
questions.takeaway: Swap files = Virtual memory files written to disk when RAM is full; Forensically valuable for recovering in-memory data

questions.question: When shutting down a computer, what information is typically lost?
options: Running processes
Data in RAM memory
All of the above
Current network connections
questions.explanation: All volatile data is lost on shutdown: RAM contents (including encryption keys, passwords, running code), running processes (their state and memory), and current network connections (open sockets and established sessions). This is why live forensics/memory acquisition must be performed before shutting down a suspect system.
questions.takeaway: System shutdown loses: RAM data + running processes + network connections — always capture volatile data FIRST

questions.question: ________________ is the science of hiding messages in messages.
options: Steganalysis
Scanning
Spoofing
Steganography
questions.explanation: Steganography (from Greek: 'concealed writing') is the science of hiding secret messages within other messages or media files, such that the hidden message's existence is not apparent. Steganalysis is the counterpart — the science of detecting steganographic content.
questions.takeaway: Steganography = Hiding messages within other messages/media; Steganalysis = Detecting hidden steganographic content

questions.question: If the Internet History file has been deleted, ____________ may still provide information about what Web sites the user has visited.
options: Cookies
Metadata
User profiles
Sessions
questions.explanation: Cookies are small text files stored by websites on a user's computer to track sessions, preferences, and browsing behavior. Even when browser history is deleted, cookie files may remain on disk and can reveal which websites a user visited, providing forensic evidence of web activity.
questions.takeaway: Cookies survive history deletion — forensically reveal visited websites even after history is cleared

questions.question: Which tool is used for seizure and acquisition in computer forensics?
options: Trueback
Cofee
Hasher
AOPR
questions.explanation: COFEE (Computer Online Forensic Evidence Extractor) is a Microsoft tool used by law enforcement for live forensic acquisition from running Windows systems. It automates the extraction of volatile evidence including processes, network connections, and registry data from a live system via USB.
questions.takeaway: COFEE = Microsoft's Computer Online Forensic Evidence Extractor; used for live Windows forensic acquisition

questions.question: Packet capturing in a network can be done by:
options: Both a and b
NeSA
Wireshark
None of the above
questions.explanation: Wireshark is the industry-standard open-source network packet analyzer (packet sniffer) used for capturing and analyzing network traffic in real-time. It captures packets from network interfaces and is widely used in network forensics for traffic analysis and intrusion investigation.
questions.takeaway: Wireshark = Industry-standard tool for network packet capture and analysis in network forensics

questions.question: Which cyber forensics tool is used for forensic analysis?
options: NeSA
Cyberinvestigator
Wireshark
Cybercheck
questions.explanation: Cybercheck is a dedicated cyber forensics analysis tool used by investigators for comprehensive digital forensic analysis including email tracing, IP location, and digital evidence examination. NeSA (Network Security Analysis) is used for network analysis, while Wireshark focuses on packet capture.
questions.takeaway: Cybercheck = Forensic analysis tool; NeSA = Network analysis; Wireshark = Packet capture

questions.question: The geographic region and email sender is identified by:
options: Email-Tracer
Wireshark
Cybercheck
NeSA
questions.explanation: Email-Tracer is a forensic tool that traces email headers to identify the geographic region and origin of an email sender by analyzing IP addresses embedded in email headers. It can trace the route of an email across mail servers to identify the originating location.
questions.takeaway: Email-Tracer = Tool for tracing email sender geographic location by analyzing email header IP addresses

questions.question: Static Analysis is also known as:
options: Disk Forensics
Traditional Forensics
Live Forensics
Computer Forensics
questions.explanation: Static Analysis (also called Dead Analysis or Traditional Forensics) is the examination of a system that is powered off. It involves analyzing disk images, files, and other non-volatile storage. This contrasts with Live (Dynamic) Forensics where the system is running and volatile data is captured.
questions.takeaway: Static Analysis = Traditional Forensics on powered-off systems; Live Forensics = Analysis on running systems

questions.question: The procedure for Network forensics includes:
options: Copy
Either a or b
Both a and b
Capture
questions.explanation: Network forensics involves both capturing and copying network traffic. The capture phase involves collecting live network packets using tools like Wireshark, while the copy phase involves preserving captured traffic for offline analysis. Both steps are essential to the network forensics process.
questions.takeaway: Network forensics = Both capture (live traffic collection) AND copy (preservation for analysis)

questions.question: The network packets are captured through:
options: Tunnel mode
Normal mode
Transport mode
Promiscuous mode
questions.explanation: Promiscuous mode is a network interface mode where the NIC captures ALL packets on the network segment, not just those addressed to it. This is required for packet sniffing and network forensics. Normal mode only captures packets addressed to the specific host.
questions.takeaway: Promiscuous mode = NIC captures ALL network packets (not just addressed to it); required for packet sniffing

questions.question: Under Estimation Based attacks, watermarks are based on some stochastic criteria such as:
options: All of the above
maximum likelihood (ML)
minimum mean square error (MMSE)
maximum a posteriori probability (MAP)
questions.explanation: In digital watermarking, Estimation Based Attacks try to estimate and remove watermarks using statistical methods. The watermarks themselves are designed using stochastic (probabilistic) criteria including maximum likelihood (ML), maximum a posteriori probability (MAP), and minimum mean square error (MMSE) optimization.
questions.takeaway: Estimation Based Attacks on watermarks use statistical criteria: ML + MAP + MMSE — all three apply

questions.question: The portion of a disk that contains no stored data, but may contain latent data is called:
options: Swap space
Unallocated space
A cluster
RAM slack
questions.explanation: Unallocated space (also called free space) is the area on a disk not assigned to any partition or file. It may contain remnants of deleted files, previous file system structures, and other latent data. This is a primary forensic recovery area. RAM slack and file slack are different types of slack space.
questions.takeaway: Unallocated space = Disk area with no active data but potentially containing latent/deleted file remnants — key forensic target

questions.question: The state of the electronic crime scene may need to be processed by one or a combination of the following methods EXCEPT:
options: pulling the plug from the back of the computer
live acquisition of the data
immediate detachment from a network server
performing a system shutdown
questions.explanation: Immediate detachment from a network server is NOT a standard processing method for an electronic crime scene. The valid approaches include live acquisition (capturing volatile data while running), system shutdown (controlled power-off), and pulling the plug (immediate power removal to preserve state). Detaching from network may destroy evidence.
questions.takeaway: Electronic crime scene methods: Live acquisition, system shutdown, pulling plug — NOT immediate network detachment

questions.question: Fragile Watermark is used for which main application?
options: Fingerprinting
Copy Control
None of the above
Multimedia Authentication
questions.explanation: Fragile watermarks are designed to be destroyed or altered when the watermarked content is modified. Their primary application is multimedia authentication — verifying that digital content (image, video, audio) has not been tampered with since the watermark was embedded. Any modification breaks the fragile watermark.
questions.takeaway: Fragile Watermark = Used for multimedia authentication; destroyed when content is modified, proving tampering

questions.question: Which steganographic tool is a file system program that can completely hide and protect files, folders and drives by making them inaccessible?
options: Easy Protector
Backyard
Anahtar
Blindside
questions.explanation: Easy Protector is a steganographic file system tool that hides and protects files, folders, and drives by making them completely inaccessible to unauthorized users. It uses steganographic techniques to conceal the existence of protected data, making it appear non-existent to the operating system.
questions.takeaway: Easy Protector = Steganographic tool that hides entire files/folders/drives making them invisible to the OS

questions.question: Which of the following attack is based on the concept of invertible attack?
options: Geometric
Oracle
Protocol
Removal
questions.explanation: Oracle attacks (also called invertibility attacks) in watermarking exploit the invertible nature of certain watermark embedding schemes. The attacker uses the watermarking system itself as an oracle to generate a 'fake original' by inverting the watermark process, creating a document that appears to have a valid watermark for different content.
questions.takeaway: Oracle Attack = Watermark attack based on invertibility; uses the embedding system itself as an oracle

questions.question: Context-free grammars are used in this category of steganography:
options: Distortion Techniques
Spread Spectrum
Cover-generation Techniques
Transform Domain Techniques
questions.explanation: Cover-generation techniques in steganography use context-free grammars to generate natural-looking cover text (called 'mimic functions') that embeds hidden data according to grammatical rules. This creates syntactically valid text that statistically resembles normal language while encoding secret messages.
questions.takeaway: Cover-generation steganography = Uses context-free grammars to generate natural cover text with hidden data

questions.question: Data to be transmitted is divided into small pieces and each piece is allocated to a frequency channel. This happens in which category of steganography?
options: Both of these
None of these
Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum
Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum
questions.explanation: Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS) steganography divides hidden data into small pieces and allocates each piece to a different frequency channel in a pseudo-random sequence. This makes the signal appear as background noise across multiple frequencies, providing covert communication and resistance to detection.
questions.takeaway: FHSS steganography = Data split into pieces, each allocated to different frequency channels; appears as noise

questions.question: What is the most significant legal issue in computer forensics?
options: Admissibility of Evidence
Preserving Evidence
Discovery of Evidence
Seizing Evidence
questions.explanation: Admissibility of evidence is the most significant legal issue in computer forensics because even perfectly preserved and seized evidence is worthless if it cannot be presented in court. Evidence must meet legal standards for authentication, relevance, and proper handling (chain of custody) to be admissible.
questions.takeaway: Most significant legal issue in forensics = Admissibility of Evidence; must meet authentication and chain-of-custody standards

questions.question: What describes the route that evidence takes from the time you find it until the case is closed or goes to court?
options: Law of probability
Chain of custody
Rules of evidence
Policy of separation
questions.explanation: Chain of custody is the chronological documentation that records every person who has handled evidence from its collection through analysis to court presentation. It must be unbroken and documented to prove evidence has not been tampered with, making it a cornerstone of forensic investigations.
questions.takeaway: Chain of custody = Documentation tracking evidence from discovery to court; must be unbroken for legal validity

questions.question: How many characters long is the fixed-length MD5 algorithm checksum of a critical system file?
questions.explanation: MD5 (Message Digest 5) produces a 128-bit hash value, which is typically expressed as a 32-character hexadecimal string. Each hex character represents 4 bits, so 128 bits = 32 hex characters. In forensics, MD5 hashes verify evidence integrity — matching hashes confirm an unaltered copy.
questions.takeaway: MD5 hash = 128-bit value displayed as 32 hexadecimal characters; used for evidence integrity verification

questions.question: Before you are called to testify as an expert witness, what must an attorney do first?
options: Prove that the tools you used to conduct your examination are perfect
Read your curriculum vitae to the jury
Engage in damage control
Qualify you as an expert witness
questions.explanation: Before a forensic investigator can testify as an expert witness, the attorney must formally qualify them through a process called 'voir dire' — establishing their credentials, experience, education, and expertise. Without this qualification, the court will not accept their testimony as expert opinion.
questions.takeaway: Before expert testimony = Attorney must qualify you as expert witness through voir dire process

questions.question: You are contracted as a forensic investigator for a bank with four 30TB storage area networks. What method would be most efficient to acquire digital evidence?
options: Create a sparse data copy of a folder or file
Make a bit-stream disk-to-disk file
Make a bit-stream disk-to-image file
Create a compressed copy of the file with DoubleSpace
questions.explanation: For extremely large storage (like 30TB SANs), creating a sparse data copy of specific folders or files is most efficient. A full bit-stream copy of 4 x 30TB = 120TB would be impractical in terms of time and storage. Sparse acquisition targets relevant allocated data, making it practical for large-scale enterprise investigations.
questions.takeaway: Large storage forensics = Sparse data copy most efficient; full bit-stream impractical for multi-TB systems

questions.question: What file structure database would you expect to find on floppy disks?
options: NTFS
FAT12
FAT16
FAT32
questions.explanation: FAT12 (12-bit File Allocation Table) was the file system used on floppy disks due to their small capacity (720KB to 1.44MB). The 12-bit FAT could address the limited number of clusters on floppy disks. FAT16 was for hard drives up to 2GB, FAT32 for larger drives, NTFS for modern Windows systems.
questions.takeaway: FAT12 = Floppy disk file system; FAT16 = Early HDD; FAT32 = Larger HDD; NTFS = Modern Windows

questions.question: What type of attack occurs when an attacker forces a router to stop forwarding packets by flooding it with many open connections simultaneously?
options: Physical Attack
ARP redirect
Denial of Service
Digital Attack
questions.explanation: A Denial of Service (DoS) attack overwhelms a target (router, server, network device) by flooding it with excessive requests or open connections, exhausting its resources and preventing it from serving legitimate traffic. All hosts behind an overwhelmed router become effectively unreachable.
questions.takeaway: DoS attack = Overwhelming target with connections/requests to exhaust resources and deny legitimate service

questions.question: When examining a file with a Hex Editor, what space does the file header occupy?
options: The last several bytes of the file
The first several bytes of the file
None, file headers are contained in the FAT
One byte at the beginning of the file
questions.explanation: File headers (magic numbers/file signatures) occupy the first byte(s) at the beginning of a file. For example, executable files (EXE) start with 'MZ' (4D 5A), PDF files start with '%PDF' (25 50 44 46). Forensic investigators use hex editors to read these header bytes to identify file types regardless of their extensions.
questions.takeaway: File header = First byte(s) at start of file identifying file type; used forensically to detect disguised files

questions.question: In the context of file deletion, which of the following statement holds true?
options: While booting, the machine may create temporary files that can delete evidence
Secure delete programs work by completely overwriting the file in one go
The longer a disk is in use, the less likely it is that deleted files will be overwritten
When files are deleted, the data is overwritten and the cluster marked as available
questions.explanation: During the boot process, the OS creates temporary files (temp files, swap files) that may be written to unallocated space, potentially overwriting deleted file evidence. The longer a disk is in use, the MORE likely deleted data will be overwritten (opposite of option B). Secure delete programs typically overwrite multiple times.
questions.takeaway: Forensic risk: OS temp files created during boot can overwrite deleted evidence in unallocated space

questions.question: A suspect cleared browser history, emptied cookie cache, and removed downloaded images. What can the investigator do to prove the violation?
options: Seek the help of co-workers who are eye-witnesses
Image the disk and try to recover deleted files
Approach the websites for evidence
Check the Windows registry for connection data
questions.explanation: The most forensically sound and feasible option is to create a disk image and use forensic tools to recover deleted files from unallocated space and file slack. Even after deletion and cache clearing, data remnants often remain on disk. Registry artifacts may also help but option A directly targets the deleted evidence.
questions.takeaway: Best approach for deleted browser evidence = Image disk and recover from unallocated space using forensic tools

questions.question: A(n) ______ is one that's performed by a computer program rather than the attacker manually performing the steps in the attack sequence.
options: Central Processing Attack
Distributed Attack
Automated Attack
Blackout Attack
questions.explanation: An Automated Attack uses scripts, malware, or programs to execute attack sequences without requiring the attacker to manually perform each step. This allows attacks to execute faster, at scale, and simultaneously against multiple targets. Most modern cyberattacks are partially or fully automated.
questions.takeaway: Automated Attack = Computer program executes attack steps automatically without manual attacker intervention

questions.question: The offset in hexadecimal code is:
options: The 0x at the end of the code
The first byte after the colon
The last byte after the colon
The 0x at the beginning of the code
questions.explanation: In hexadecimal notation, the prefix '0x' at the beginning of a value indicates that the number is in base-16 (hexadecimal) format. The offset in hex editors refers to the memory address or file position, typically represented with the '0x' prefix to distinguish from decimal values.
questions.takeaway: Hexadecimal offset = 0x prefix at the beginning indicates base-16 (hex) value; e.g., 0x1A = 26 decimal

questions.question: It takes _____________ mismanaged case(s) to ruin your professional reputation as a computer forensics examiner?
options: only one
by law, three
quite a few
at least two
questions.explanation: In computer forensics, professional reputation is paramount because testimony and findings can determine criminal outcomes. A single mismanaged case — through improper evidence handling, incorrect analysis, or ethical violations — can permanently damage credibility in court and end a forensic examiner's career.
questions.takeaway: Forensic examiner reputation = Only ONE mismanaged case needed to permanently damage professional credibility

questions.question: With the standard Linux second extended file system (EXT2fs), a file is deleted when the inode internal link count reaches ________.
questions.explanation: In Linux EXT2/EXT3 file systems, each file has an inode containing metadata. The inode maintains a link count — the number of directory entries pointing to it. When a file is deleted, its directory entry is removed and the link count decremented. When the link count reaches 0, the inode is marked free and data blocks are released.
questions.takeaway: EXT2fs file deletion = Occurs when inode link count reaches 0; data blocks marked free but not immediately erased

questions.question: An employee is attempting to wipe data from CDs and DVDs using a large magnet. Why will this not work?
options: logical
anti-magnetic
optical
magnetic
questions.explanation: CDs and DVDs use optical technology — data is encoded as microscopic pits and lands on a reflective layer using laser technology. Magnetic fields have no effect on optical media because optical data is not stored magnetically. Only magnetic storage (HDDs, floppy disks, magnetic tape) can be erased with magnets.
questions.takeaway: CDs/DVDs = Optical media; magnets cannot erase them; only magnetic storage (HDD, floppy) is affected by magnets

questions.question: What does the acronym POST mean as it relates to a PC?
options: Primary Operating System Test
Primary Operations Short Test
Pre Operational Situation Test
Power On Self Test
questions.explanation: POST (Power On Self Test) is a diagnostic testing sequence run by BIOS/UEFI firmware when a computer powers on. It checks hardware components (CPU, RAM, storage, peripherals) before handing control to the OS. Forensically, POST behavior can indicate hardware issues during evidence collection.
questions.takeaway: POST = Power On Self Test; BIOS/UEFI hardware diagnostic run at computer startup before OS loads

questions.question: Which legal document allows law enforcement to search an office, place of business, or other locale for evidence relating to an alleged crime?
options: Search warrant
Subpoena
Wire Tap
Bench warrant
questions.explanation: A search warrant is a court order authorizing law enforcement to enter and search a specific location for specific evidence. It must specify the place to be searched and items to be seized. In digital forensics, search warrants authorize seizure of computers and digital devices as evidence.
questions.takeaway: Search warrant = Court order authorizing search of location and seizure of specified evidence items

questions.question: You are collecting 15 hard drives as evidence. Your manager instructs you to complete one multi-evidence form and a single-evidence form for each drive. How will these forms be stored?
options: Multi-evidence form in secure container with drives; single-evidence forms in report file
Multi-evidence form in report file; single-evidence forms with each drive in secure container
All forms in report file as primary evidence
All forms placed in an approved secure container as primary evidence
questions.explanation: Proper chain of custody practice: the multi-evidence form (covering the entire case) goes in the report file for documentation purposes, while individual single-evidence forms accompany each hard drive in the approved secure container. This links each piece of physical evidence to its documentation.
questions.takeaway: Evidence forms: Multi-evidence form → report file; Single-evidence forms → with each drive in secure container

questions.question: The MD5 program is used to:
options: make directories on an evidence disk
view graphics files on an evidence drive
verify that a disk is not altered when you examine it
wipe magnetic media before recycling it
questions.explanation: MD5 (Message Digest 5) is used in forensics to create a cryptographic hash (fingerprint) of a disk or file. By comparing the MD5 hash of the original evidence with the hash of the forensic copy, investigators can prove the copy is identical and unaltered — fundamental to evidence integrity.
questions.takeaway: MD5 in forensics = Creates hash fingerprint to verify disk/file integrity; proves forensic copy is unaltered

questions.question: Which is the standard procedure to perform during all computer forensics investigations regarding system time?
options: With drive removed, check time in system's RAM
With drive in suspect PC, check time in File Allocation Table
With drive in suspect PC, check time in system's CMOS
With drive removed from suspect PC, check time in system's CMOS
questions.explanation: Standard forensic procedure requires checking the CMOS clock with the hard drive REMOVED from the suspect PC. This ensures the time comes from the hardware clock, not the OS (which could be manipulated). The time discrepancy between CMOS and actual time must be documented for timeline accuracy.
questions.takeaway: Forensic time check = Remove drive, check CMOS clock; document time discrepancy for accurate event timeline

questions.question: In a forensic examination, what type of user is most likely to have the most file slack to analyze?
options: One who has lots of allocation units per block or cluster
One who has NTFS 4 or 5 partitions
One who uses dynamic swap file capability
One who uses hard disk writes on IRQ 13 and 21
questions.explanation: File slack is the unused space within the last allocated cluster of a file (between end of file data and end of the cluster). Users with large cluster sizes (many allocation units per cluster) generate more file slack per file, because small files still consume entire large clusters. More slack = more potential forensic evidence.
questions.takeaway: More file slack = Larger cluster sizes; users with large allocation units per cluster have most slack space for analysis

questions.question: While working on a high-profile criminal case, your boss worries the defense attorney may claim evidence was changed. How do you prove evidence integrity?
options: No need to worry as state labs are certified
Make an MD5 hash and compare with original MD5 hash from evidence intake
Make an MD5 hash and compare to NIST standard database
Sign a statement attesting evidence is unchanged
questions.explanation: To prove evidence hasn't been changed in the lab, compare the current MD5 hash of the evidence with the MD5 hash taken when evidence first entered the lab. Matching hashes mathematically prove the evidence is bit-for-bit identical — no changes occurred. This is the forensic gold standard for integrity verification.
questions.takeaway: Evidence integrity proof = Compare current MD5 hash with intake MD5 hash; matching = proven unaltered

questions.question: What is the name of the service used to synchronize time among multiple computers for forensic investigations?
options: Universal Time Set
Time-Sync Protocol
Network Time Protocol
SyncTime Service
questions.explanation: Network Time Protocol (NTP) is the standard protocol for synchronizing clocks across networked computers. In forensics, synchronized time is critical for correlating log entries across multiple systems to reconstruct the exact sequence of events during an attack or incident.
questions.takeaway: NTP = Network Time Protocol; critical for forensic timeline correlation across multiple systems

questions.question: When investigating a potential e-mail crime, what is your first step?
options: Write a report
Determine whether a crime was actually committed
Recover the evidence
Trace the IP address to its origin
questions.explanation: The first step in email crime investigation is tracing the IP address found in email headers back to its origin. This identifies the source of the email (sender's ISP, geographic location) and determines jurisdiction. Email headers contain the sender's IP address, relay servers, and timestamps.
questions.takeaway: Email crime investigation first step = Trace IP address from email headers to identify sender's origin

questions.question: If a suspect computer is located in an area that may have toxic chemicals, you must:
options: Coordinate with the HAZMAT team
Determine a way to obtain the suspect computer
Assume the suspect machine is contaminated
Do not enter alone
questions.explanation: Investigator safety is paramount. When a suspect computer is in an area with potentially toxic chemicals, the first action is to coordinate with the HAZMAT (Hazardous Materials) team. They will assess the risk, provide appropriate PPE, and ensure the area is safe before investigators collect evidence.
questions.takeaway: Toxic chemical environment = Coordinate with HAZMAT team first; investigator safety supersedes evidence collection

questions.question: What happens when a file is deleted by a Microsoft operating system using the FAT file system?
options: A copy of the file is stored and the original file is erased
Only the reference to the file is removed from the FAT
The file is erased and cannot be recovered
The file is erased but can be recovered
questions.explanation: When a file is deleted in a FAT file system, Windows only removes the reference (directory entry and FAT cluster chain) to the file. The actual data remains on disk until overwritten by new data. This is why deleted files can be forensically recovered using tools like Recuva, FTK, or EnCase.
questions.takeaway: FAT file deletion = Only reference removed; actual data remains until overwritten — forensically recoverable

questions.question: What term describes a cryptographic technique for embedding information into something else for the sole purpose of hiding that information from the casual observer?
options: key escrow
rootkit
steganography
offset
questions.explanation: Steganography (Greek: 'concealed writing') is the technique of embedding hidden information within ordinary files (images, audio, video, text) so that the presence of the hidden message is not apparent to casual observers. This distinguishes it from cryptography, which makes data unreadable but visible.
questions.takeaway: Steganography = Hiding information existence within cover media; different from encryption which hides content not existence

questions.question: During an investigation, you find evidence that may prove the suspect's innocence. You report this. This type of evidence is known as:
options: Inculpatory evidence
Mandatory evidence
Terrible evidence
Exculpatory evidence
questions.explanation: Exculpatory evidence is evidence that tends to show a suspect's innocence or reduces their culpability. Forensic investigators have an ethical and legal obligation to report exculpatory evidence. This contrasts with inculpatory evidence (which supports guilt). A forensic examiner must remain objective and impartial.
questions.takeaway: Exculpatory evidence = Evidence proving innocence; must be reported; contrasts with Inculpatory (guilt-proving) evidence

questions.question: What binary coding is used most often for e-mail purposes?
options: MIME
Uuencode
IMAP
SMTP
questions.explanation: MIME (Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions) is the standard encoding used for email to support non-ASCII characters, attachments, and multimedia content. It extends the basic email format (RFC 822) to allow binary data (images, documents) to be transmitted. IMAP and SMTP are protocols, not encoding formats.
questions.takeaway: MIME = Standard email encoding for attachments and multimedia; SMTP = sending protocol; IMAP = retrieval protocol

questions.question: What does the Superblock in Linux define?
options: Available space
Location of the first inode
Disk geometry
File system names
questions.explanation: The Superblock in Linux ext2/ext3/ext4 file systems is a critical data structure that contains metadata about the entire file system including: block size, number of blocks, number of inodes, free block count, and the location of the first inode (inode table start). It also records disk geometry and available space.
questions.takeaway: Linux Superblock = Contains file system metadata including location of first inode, block counts, and free space

questions.question: ________ identifies the trustees that are allowed or denied access to a securable object.
options: UACL
SACL
DACL
TACL
questions.explanation: DACL (Discretionary Access Control List) is part of Windows security descriptors that specifies which users or groups are allowed or denied access to a securable object (file, registry key, process). SACL (System ACL) is used for auditing — logging access attempts rather than controlling them.
questions.takeaway: DACL = Discretionary ACL; controls WHO can access objects; SACL = System ACL; logs/audits access attempts

questions.question: _______ enables administrators to log attempts to access a secured object in Windows.
options: UACL
DACL
TACL
SACL
questions.explanation: SACL (System Access Control List) is the Windows security component that enables auditing of object access. When SACL entries are configured, Windows logs security events when users access (or attempt to access) objects like files, registry keys, and printers. These logs are forensically valuable.
questions.takeaway: SACL = System ACL; enables auditing of object access attempts; forensically valuable for access trail

questions.question: The ______ setting is used to audit each event that is related to a user logging on to, logging off from, or making a network connection to the computer.
options: Audit logon events
Audit account logon events
Audit account management
Audit directory service access
questions.explanation: Audit Logon Events policy tracks actual logon/logoff events on the local computer, including interactive logons and network connections. This differs from 'Audit account logon events' which tracks credential validation. For forensic timeline reconstruction, audit logon events reveal when users accessed a system.
questions.takeaway: Audit Logon Events = Tracks local logon/logoff and network connections; key for forensic user activity timeline

questions.question: On a Linux machine, the _______ is responsible for writing audit records to the disk.
options: syslogd daemon
auditd daemon
log daemon
track daemon
questions.explanation: The auditd (Linux Audit Daemon) is the userspace component of the Linux Audit framework responsible for receiving audit records from the kernel and writing them to disk. It manages audit log files, handles log rotation, and ensures audit records are preserved for forensic investigation.
questions.takeaway: auditd = Linux Audit Daemon; writes kernel audit records to disk; manages audit log files

questions.question: The _____ command is used to assist controlling the kernel's audit system in Linux.
options: aureport
ausearch
auditctl
syslogd
questions.explanation: auditctl is the Linux command-line tool used to configure and control the Linux kernel's audit system. It allows administrators to set audit rules, watch files/directories for access, and configure audit parameters. Example: 'auditctl -w /etc/passwd -p wa' watches the passwd file for write/attribute changes.
questions.takeaway: auditctl = Linux command to configure kernel audit system and set audit rules/watches

questions.question: The _______ command is used to set an audit watch on a file in Linux.
options: ausearch
aureport
syslogd
auditctl
questions.explanation: auditctl with the -w flag is used to set audit watches on specific files or directories. For example: 'auditctl -w /etc/shadow -p rwa' watches the shadow password file for read, write, and attribute changes. This enables forensic monitoring of sensitive file access.
questions.takeaway: auditctl -w = Sets audit watch on a file; monitors read/write/execute/attribute changes for forensic logging

questions.question: _______ is a Linux command that can query the audit daemon logs for events based on different search criteria.
options: ausearch
search
logsearch
syslogd
questions.explanation: ausearch is a Linux command-line utility that searches and filters the binary audit log files maintained by auditd. It supports filtering by user, time, event type, system call, and other criteria. Forensic investigators use ausearch to find specific security events in audit logs.
questions.takeaway: ausearch = Linux tool to search/filter audit logs by user, time, event type; essential for forensic log analysis

questions.question: ______ is a Linux tool that produces summary reports of the audit system logs.
options: syslogd
aureport
ausearch
auditctl
questions.explanation: aureport generates formatted summary reports from the Linux audit logs. It can produce reports on user logins, failed authentications, system calls, file accesses, and other audit events. This gives forensic investigators a high-level overview of system activity over time.
questions.takeaway: aureport = Generates summary reports from Linux audit logs; provides activity overview for forensic analysis

questions.question: ETW stands for:
options: Entry Tracking for Windows
Engress Tracking for Windows
Event Tracing for Windows
Event Tracking for Windows
questions.explanation: ETW (Event Tracing for Windows) is a high-performance, kernel-level event tracing mechanism built into Windows. It enables logging of system and application events with minimal overhead. ETW is widely used in forensic analysis and security tools (like Sysmon, Windows Defender) to track system activity.
questions.takeaway: ETW = Event Tracing for Windows; kernel-level event logging mechanism used by forensic and security tools

questions.question: In Kerberos, TGT is the short form for:
options: Token Granting Terminal
Token Granting Ticket
Ticket Granting Terminal
Ticket Granting Ticket
questions.explanation: TGT (Ticket Granting Ticket) is issued by the Kerberos Authentication Server (AS) after successful initial authentication. The TGT is presented to the Ticket Granting Server (TGS) to obtain Service Tickets for accessing specific network resources. Forensically, stolen TGTs enable 'Pass-the-Ticket' attacks.
questions.takeaway: TGT = Ticket Granting Ticket in Kerberos; obtained after authentication; used to get service tickets

questions.question: If you come across a sheepdip machine at a client site, what would you infer?
options: A sheepdip computer is used only for virus-checking.
A sheepdip computer defers a denial of service attack
A sheepdip coordinates several honeypots
A sheepdip computer is another name for a honeypot
questions.explanation: A sheepdip (or sheep dip) machine is an isolated computer used exclusively for virus-checking and malware analysis of suspicious media (USB drives, floppy disks, CDs) before they enter a network. It is physically isolated from the network to prevent potential malware spread during analysis.
questions.takeaway: Sheepdip machine = Isolated computer used only for virus-checking suspicious media before network entry

questions.question: Which part of the Windows Registry contains the user's password file?
options: HKEY_CURRENT_USER
HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE
HKEY_USER
HKEY_CURRENT_CONFIGURATION
questions.explanation: The Windows SAM (Security Account Manager) database, which contains hashed user passwords, is stored under HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SAM in the Windows Registry. This hive is normally locked during system operation and requires special forensic techniques (like booting from external media) to access.
questions.takeaway: Windows password hashes = HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE (SAM hive); locked during normal operation

questions.question: Lance wants to place a honeypot on his network. What would be the recommended placement?
options: Use it on a system in an external DMZ in front of the firewall
Use a system that has dynamic addressing on the network
It doesn't matter as all replies are faked
Use a system that is not directly interfacing with the router
questions.explanation: Honeypots are deception systems that appear to be legitimate targets to attract attackers. The placement specifics are flexible because the honeypot software fabricates all replies to simulate a real system regardless of network position. The specific configuration matters more than placement.
questions.takeaway: Honeypot placement flexibility = All replies are simulated/faked; software creates the illusion regardless of position

questions.question: Which legal document allows an investigator to contact an ISP by telephone to preserve e-mail records, as authorized by U.S.C. statute?
options: Title 18, Section 2703(d)
Title 18, Section Chapter 90
Title 18, Section 1030
Title 18, Section 2703(f)
questions.explanation: Title 18, Section 2703(f) of the U.S. Code (Electronic Communications Privacy Act) authorizes law enforcement to contact a provider by telephone (without a court order) and request preservation of electronic communications records. The ISP is then obligated to preserve those records for up to 90 days.
questions.takeaway: 18 U.S.C. 2703(f) = Authorizes telephone request to ISP to preserve email records; ISP obligated to comply

questions.question: The newer Macintosh Operating System is based on:
options: BSD Unix
Linux
OS/2
Microsoft Windows
questions.explanation: macOS (formerly Mac OS X) is based on Darwin, which itself is built on BSD Unix (specifically a combination of Mach kernel and FreeBSD). This Unix foundation gives macOS POSIX compliance and Unix-like command-line capabilities. Forensically, macOS investigations use Unix forensic techniques.
questions.takeaway: macOS = Based on BSD Unix (Darwin kernel); POSIX-compliant; Unix forensic techniques apply

questions.question: Which method is used for gathering forensic evidence?
options: Imaging
Acquisitioning
Preserving
Copying
questions.explanation: Acquisition (acquisitioning) is the formal forensic process of collecting and creating verified copies of digital evidence using write-blocked, hash-verified methods. It includes creating bit-stream images while maintaining the integrity of the original evidence through write blockers and hash verification.
questions.takeaway: Acquisition = Formal process of collecting digital evidence using write-blocked, hash-verified bit-stream methods

questions.question: Which of the following attack is also called an Oracle attack?
options: Both A and B
None of the above
Black Box Attack
White Box Attack
questions.explanation: A Black Box Attack (also called an Oracle attack in watermarking context) is performed without internal knowledge of the system, treating it as a 'black box' — an oracle that provides output for given inputs. The attacker queries the system and uses responses to infer information about internal workings.
questions.takeaway: Black Box Attack = Also known as Oracle attack; no internal system knowledge; treats system as input-output oracle

questions.question: Which of the following falls into the category of Representative attacks in digital watermarking?
options: Gradient Attack
Collusion Attack
Scrambling Attacks
All of the above
questions.explanation: Representative attacks in digital watermarking include all methods that attempt to create a non-watermarked version of content: Scrambling Attacks (reorder content), Collusion Attacks (average multiple watermarked copies), and Gradient Attacks (use derivative information to remove watermarks). All three are representative attack types.
questions.takeaway: Representative attacks on watermarks: Scrambling + Collusion + Gradient — all three are valid attack types

questions.question: Process of responding to a computer-related incident and methodically securing, preserving, and documenting digital evidence using a prescribed methodology is called:
options: Incident Response
Forensics Investigation
Forensics Analysis
Incident Report
questions.explanation: Incident Response is the systematic process of responding to security incidents (cybercrime, policy violations) by methodically securing, preserving, and documenting digital evidence. It follows a prescribed methodology (preparation, identification, containment, eradication, recovery, lessons learned) to ensure legal defensibility.
questions.takeaway: Incident Response = Systematic process of securing and preserving digital evidence following a prescribed methodology

questions.question: A group of highly skilled individuals in computer forensics and incident responding, with alternate duties in a corporate or legal environment, is called:
options: Incident Response Technician Repair Team
Incident Response Team
Corporate Forensics Team
Forensics Investigation Team
questions.explanation: An Incident Response Team (IRT) is a group of professionals with expertise in computer forensics and incident handling who respond to security incidents. In organizations, team members often have regular job functions but come together when incidents occur — making it an 'alternate duty' team.
questions.takeaway: IRT = Incident Response Team; skilled forensics/security professionals with alternate duties in corporate environments

questions.question: The IRT reports to:
options: Information Security Operations Manager
IT Manager
Top Management
Network Manager
questions.explanation: The Incident Response Team (IRT) reports to Top Management (executive leadership) because security incidents may have legal, financial, and reputational consequences requiring executive decision-making authority. Top management provides authorization for major response actions and communicates with stakeholders.
questions.takeaway: IRT reports to Top Management — incidents have executive-level implications requiring leadership oversight

questions.question: The ___________ is used to document location and status of evidence at all times.
options: Incident Response Documentation
Forensic Analysis Report
Acceptable Usage Policy
Chain of Custody
questions.explanation: The Chain of Custody document records the location, handling, and status of evidence at every point from collection to court presentation. It documents who collected the evidence, when, where, how it was stored, and who accessed it. Any gap in the chain of custody can make evidence inadmissible.
questions.takeaway: Chain of Custody = Document tracking evidence location, status, and handlers at ALL times throughout investigation

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a type of evidence?
options: None of the above
Direct
Demonstrative
Circumstantial
questions.explanation: Direct, Circumstantial, and Demonstrative are all recognized types of legal evidence. Direct evidence (eyewitness testimony), Circumstantial evidence (indirect proof through inference), and Demonstrative evidence (physical models, diagrams). 'None of the above' is the answer because all three listed ARE valid evidence types.
questions.takeaway: Evidence types: Direct + Circumstantial + Demonstrative — all are valid; there is no 'not a type' among these

questions.question: Actual evidence obtained at a crime scene is classified as:
options: Circumstantial
Direct
Documentary
Testimonial
questions.explanation: Direct evidence is actual physical evidence directly proving a fact without requiring inference — such as digital devices, files, or artifacts recovered from a crime scene. Circumstantial evidence requires inference; Testimonial is witness statements; Documentary is documents. Physical crime scene evidence is Direct.
questions.takeaway: Direct evidence = Actual physical evidence from crime scene proving facts directly without inference

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a disk duplication type in computer forensics?
options: Partition Duplication
Forensic Duplication
Volume Duplication
Simple File Copying
questions.explanation: Volume Duplication is not a standard recognized disk duplication type in computer forensics. The recognized types are: Simple File Copying (copies files, misses deleted data), Partition Duplication (copies partition), and Forensic Duplication (bit-stream copy of entire drive including unallocated space). Volume is not a standard category.
questions.takeaway: Disk duplication types: Simple File Copy, Partition Duplication, Forensic Duplication — Volume Duplication is NOT standard

questions.question: ___________  creates a 'fingerprint' of data so that it can be compared to another copy of the data to determine if it has been altered.
options: Imaging
Digital verification
Encryption
Hashing
questions.explanation: Hashing (using algorithms like MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256) creates a fixed-length digital fingerprint of data. Any change to even a single bit of the original data produces a completely different hash value. This allows forensic investigators to verify that evidence copies are identical to originals.
questions.takeaway: Hashing = Creates unique data fingerprint; any modification changes the hash; proves data integrity

questions.question: NTFS uses ______________ to manage the file system.
options: Master File Table (MFT)
Reference File Table (RFT)
File Allocation Table (FAT)
New Technology File Table (NTFT)
questions.explanation: NTFS (New Technology File System) uses the Master File Table (MFT) to track all files and directories on a volume. The MFT contains a record for every file including metadata (name, size, timestamps, permissions) and pointers to file data. Forensically, the MFT is critical for file system analysis and deleted file recovery.
questions.takeaway: NTFS = Uses Master File Table (MFT) to track all files; key forensic artifact for file analysis

questions.question: SHA-1 is _________________.
options: 128 bit
160 bit
64 bit
256 bit
questions.explanation: SHA-1 (Secure Hash Algorithm 1) produces a 160-bit (20-byte) hash value, typically displayed as a 40-character hexadecimal string. In forensics, SHA-1 was widely used alongside MD5 for evidence integrity verification, though SHA-256 is now preferred as SHA-1 has known collision vulnerabilities.
questions.takeaway: SHA-1 = 160-bit hash (40 hex chars); used in forensics for evidence integrity; superseded by SHA-256

questions.question: In Digital Forensics, the unused space in a cluster is known as ___________________.
options: Reserved Space
Digital Space
Slack Space
Cluster Space
questions.explanation: Slack space is the unused area within the last allocated cluster of a file. When a file doesn't completely fill its last cluster, the remaining space is called file slack. It may contain fragments of previously stored data. RAM slack (from the end of file data to sector boundary) and file slack are both forensically valuable.
questions.takeaway: Slack Space = Unused area in last cluster of a file; may contain remnants of previous data; forensically valuable

questions.question: What principles guide your behavior towards people in an ethical context?
options: Customs
Legal principles
Norms
Ethics
questions.explanation: Ethics are the moral principles that govern a person's behavior and decision-making, particularly regarding what is right or wrong in dealings with others. While norms, customs, and legal principles also influence behavior, ethics specifically addresses the moral dimension of conduct in professional and personal contexts.
questions.takeaway: Ethics = Moral principles governing behavior; distinguishable from norms (social), customs (traditional), and legal rules

questions.question: Which tracking program records every keystroke you make on the computer?
options: Key logger
eFollow
ITrack/ULead
Stalking software
questions.explanation: A keylogger is software (or hardware) that silently records every keystroke made on a computer keyboard. Keyloggers capture passwords, credit card numbers, private messages, and other sensitive information. They are both used in cybercrime and by forensic investigators to gather evidence of computer use.
questions.takeaway: Keylogger = Records every keystroke; captures passwords and sensitive data; tool used in both attacks and forensics

questions.question: What is it called when you are rerouted from your requested internet site to another, undesired site?
options: Redirecting
Pharming
Hijacking
Phishing
questions.explanation: Pharming is a cyberattack where users are redirected from a legitimate website to a fraudulent one without their knowledge, typically by poisoning DNS caches or modifying hosts files. This is more dangerous than phishing because users don't click malicious links — they are redirected even when typing correct URLs.
questions.takeaway: Pharming = Redirecting users to fake websites by DNS poisoning or hosts file manipulation; more insidious than phishing

questions.question: What is the term that refers to an ad hidden inside software that you downloaded from an internet site?
options: Cookie
Phish
Adware
Spam
questions.explanation: Adware is software that automatically displays or downloads advertising material (banners, pop-ups) bundled with free software. While not always malicious, adware can track browsing behavior and compromise privacy. Forensically, adware installation may indicate unauthorized software installation or policy violations.
questions.takeaway: Adware = Advertising software bundled with free downloads; displays ads and may track user behavior

questions.question: What type of software secretly collects information about you and your internet habits?
options: Spam
Pharming
Detectware
Spyware
questions.explanation: Spyware is malicious software that covertly collects user information (browsing habits, keystrokes, passwords, personal data) and sends it to a remote party without the user's consent. It operates silently in the background and can be used for identity theft, corporate espionage, or surveillance.
questions.takeaway: Spyware = Covertly collects user data and transmits to attacker; differs from adware (shows ads) and spam (unsolicited mail)

questions.question: What records information about your visit to a website including ads watched, length of stay, and purchases?
options: Clickstream
Spyware
Pharming
Logger
questions.explanation: A clickstream is a record of a user's web browsing activity — the sequence of hyperlinks followed during a web session, including pages visited, time spent, ads viewed, and purchases made. Clickstream data is valuable forensically for reconstructing a user's online activity.
questions.takeaway: Clickstream = Record of all web activity including pages visited, time spent, ads viewed, and purchases

questions.question: Which type of law allows people to use copyrighted material in teaching environments without obtaining the consent of the copyright owner?
options: Patent
Fair Use Doctrine
Intellectual property
Copyright
questions.explanation: The Fair Use Doctrine is a legal principle in copyright law that allows limited use of copyrighted material without permission for purposes such as education, commentary, criticism, research, and news reporting. It balances copyright protection with the public interest in information access.
questions.takeaway: Fair Use Doctrine = Permits limited copyrighted material use for education, commentary, research without permission

questions.question: What type of access authentication system uses your physical characteristics to determine access rights?
options: Behaviometrics
Human system recognition
Biometrics
Physiological systems
questions.explanation: Biometrics is the authentication technology that verifies identity using unique physical or behavioral characteristics: fingerprints, facial recognition, iris scans, voice patterns, or gait analysis. Biometric systems are increasingly used in security-sensitive environments because they are difficult to forge.
questions.takeaway: Biometrics = Authentication using unique physical characteristics (fingerprint, iris, face); cannot be forgotten or stolen

questions.question: What category of software is built to harm your computer?
options: Network snakes
Systems software
Bugs
Malware
questions.explanation: Malware (malicious software) is the umbrella term for any software intentionally designed to disrupt, damage, or gain unauthorized access to computers. It includes viruses, worms, trojans, ransomware, spyware, and adware. Understanding malware types is fundamental to cyber forensics investigation.
questions.takeaway: Malware = Umbrella term for all malicious software (viruses, worms, trojans, ransomware, spyware)

questions.question: What worm emailed people with the words 'I LOVE YOU' in the year 2000?
options: The Love Bug
The Darling Link
The Love Letter
The Valentine Connection
questions.explanation: The ILOVEYOU virus (Love Bug) was a computer worm that spread in May 2000 via email with 'ILOVEYOU' in the subject line. It overwrote files and copied itself to all Outlook contacts, infecting millions of computers worldwide and causing estimated $10 billion in damages — one of the most destructive worms ever.
questions.takeaway: ILOVEYOU / Love Bug = 2000 email worm with 'ILOVEYOU' subject; overwrote files; caused ~$10B damages

questions.question: What computer program runs automatically, without your help?
options: Routines
Bot
Android
Application software
questions.explanation: A bot (short for robot) is a software program that runs automated tasks without human intervention. Malicious bots (zombies) are used in botnets for spam, DDoS attacks, and credential stuffing. Forensically, bot infections are identified through unusual automated network traffic patterns.
questions.takeaway: Bot = Automated software program running without user interaction; malicious bots form botnets for attacks

questions.question: What is it called when someone changes the FROM section of an email so the message appears to come from a different person?
options: DoS
Spam
Spoofing
Trapper
questions.explanation: Email spoofing is the manipulation of email headers to make a message appear to originate from a different sender. Attackers use spoofing in phishing attacks to impersonate trusted entities. Forensic email investigation involves examining full email headers to trace the true origin of spoofed messages.
questions.takeaway: Email spoofing = Falsifying FROM header to impersonate another sender; traced via full email header analysis

questions.question: What program would you use to gain administrative rights to someone's computer?
options: Bot
Trojan horse
Executive Android
Rootkit
questions.explanation: A rootkit is malicious software designed to gain root/administrator-level access to a system and hide its presence. Rootkits modify OS functions to conceal processes, files, and network connections from security tools. Detecting rootkits requires specialized forensic tools that operate outside the compromised OS.
questions.takeaway: Rootkit = Malware gaining admin/root access while hiding presence; requires off-OS forensic tools to detect

questions.question: What is your computer called when it is infected with a malware bot?
options: Zombie
Android
Dirty bot
E-Ogre
questions.explanation: A zombie is a computer infected by malware that allows it to be remotely controlled by an attacker (botmaster) without the owner's knowledge. Zombies are part of botnets used for DDoS attacks, spam distribution, and credential harvesting. Forensic signs include unusual outbound traffic and processes.
questions.takeaway: Zombie = Bot-infected computer controlled remotely; part of a botnet; owner unaware

questions.question: Which of the following tasks is NOT completed during the first phase of digital forensics?
options: Confiscate digital equipment and passwords
Turn on the computer and analyze its contents
Photographing the site and surrounding environment
Make a forensic image copy of all the information
questions.explanation: During the first phase (scene documentation/collection), investigators photograph, document, and seize equipment. Turning on a computer to analyze it is NOT done in the first phase — it risks altering evidence (modifying file timestamps, overwriting swap files). Analysis occurs in the lab on forensic copies.
questions.takeaway: First phase of digital forensics = Document, photograph, seize; NEVER turn on computers at scene for analysis

questions.question: In digital forensics, what is the second phase of the investigation?
options: Collection
Planning
Analysis
Authentication and preservation
questions.explanation: The second phase of digital forensics is Analysis — where forensic examiners examine the collected evidence (disk images, memory dumps, logs) to find relevant data, recover deleted files, reconstruct timelines, and identify artifacts of criminal activity. Phase 1 is collection; Phase 2 is analysis; Phase 3 is reporting.
questions.takeaway: Digital forensics phases: 1-Collection → 2-Analysis → 3-Reporting; Analysis is the second phase

questions.question: Can you recover a deleted computer file?
options: Yes, but only immediately after deletion
No
Not unless you're in safe mode
Yes, you can get it out of the recycling bin
questions.explanation: Yes — deleted files in Windows go to the Recycle Bin first, where they can be restored. Even after emptying the Recycle Bin, deleted files remain on disk (with directory entry modified) until overwritten. Forensic tools can recover files from unallocated space long after deletion and Recycle Bin emptying.
questions.takeaway: Deleted files: Recoverable from Recycle Bin; even after emptying, recoverable from unallocated space forensically

questions.question: What term depicts the blacking out of sections of a document to hide confidential information?
options: Redacting
Blackout
Whiteout
Information masking
questions.explanation: Redacting is the process of removing or obscuring sensitive or confidential information from a document before it is released or published. In digital forensics and legal proceedings, documents are often redacted to protect personal data, attorney-client privilege, or classified information.
questions.takeaway: Redacting = Blacking out/removing sensitive information from documents before disclosure; standard legal practice

questions.question: In what way do the procedures for dealing with evidence in a criminal case differ from those in a civil case?
options: Evidence procedures are not important unless for law enforcement
Evidence in a criminal case must be secured more tightly than in a civil case
Evidence in a civil case must be secured more tightly than in a criminal case
Evidence must be handled the same regardless of case type
questions.explanation: Criminal cases require more rigorous evidence handling than civil cases because: criminal convictions can result in loss of liberty, the standard of proof is higher ('beyond reasonable doubt' vs 'preponderance of evidence'), and constitutional rights (4th Amendment protections) impose stricter rules on evidence collection.
questions.takeaway: Criminal evidence = More strictly secured than civil evidence; higher burden of proof and constitutional protections apply

questions.question: From a honeypot log, what does the entry 'IDS181/nops-x86: 63.226.81.13:1351 -> 172.16.1.107:53' indicate?
options: A DNS zone transfer
Data being retrieved from 63.226.81.13
An IDS evasion technique
A buffer overflow attempt
questions.explanation: NOP sleds (no-operation instructions) are a classic IDS evasion technique used alongside buffer overflows. By prepending NOPs before shellcode, attackers can bypass signature-based IDS rules that look for specific byte patterns. The 'nops-x86' signature indicates NOP sled detection, which is an evasion technique rather than the overflow itself.
questions.takeaway: NOP sled = IDS evasion technique; prepended before shellcode to bypass signature-based detection

questions.question: What happens when a file is deleted by a Microsoft OS using the FAT file system? (CHFI variant)
options: The file is erased and cannot be recovered
A copy of the file is stored and the original is erased
The file is erased but can be recovered
Only the reference to the file is removed from the FAT
questions.explanation: In FAT file system, deleting a file only removes the directory reference and marks FAT clusters as available. The actual file data remains on disk until those clusters are overwritten by new files. This fundamental behavior makes forensic file recovery possible and is why secure deletion tools must overwrite data.
questions.takeaway: FAT deletion = Removes only the reference; data persists until overwritten — foundation of forensic file recovery

questions.question: Corporate investigations are typically easier than public investigations because:
options: The users have standard corporate equipment and software
The users can load whatever they want on their machines
The investigator does not have to get a warrant
The investigator has to get a warrant
questions.explanation: Corporate investigations are easier because: companies control the hardware and software deployed (standard builds), corporate policies (acceptable use policies) define what's allowed, and investigators are often internal or work under corporate authorization. Standard equipment means consistent forensic methodologies apply.
questions.takeaway: Corporate investigations easier = Standard equipment/software + corporate policy framework + no warrant needed with corporate authorization

questions.question: If you see the files Zer0.tar.gz and copy.tar.gz on a Linux system during an investigation, what can you conclude?
