Real-time process activity including CPU/memory consumption, sorted by resource usage
The topmost directory in the Linux file hierarchy
Network connection statistics in real time
questions.explanation: The 'top' command displays a real-time, dynamic view of running processes sorted by CPU usage. In live forensics, it helps identify: processes consuming abnormal CPU/memory (possible cryptomining malware), processes running as root unexpectedly, and process PIDs for further investigation with other tools.
questions.takeaway: top = Real-time process CPU/MEM view; identifies abnormal resource consumption; reveals potential malware activity

questions.question: What does the command 'netstat -antp' reveal in a Linux forensic investigation?
options: Network adapter hardware information
Network traffic statistics per interface
All active TCP connections, listening ports, and the processes associated with each
ARP table entries for local network devices
questions.explanation: 'netstat -antp' shows all (-a) TCP (-t) connections in numeric format (-n) with the process owning each connection (-p). Forensically, this reveals: outbound connections to suspicious IPs (malware C2), unusual listening ports (backdoors), and which process owns each connection for correlation with ps output.
questions.takeaway: netstat -antp = All TCP connections + listening ports + owning process; reveals malware C2 and backdoors

questions.question: What is syslog and what forensic information does it contain?
options: A system configuration backup tool
A network traffic logging tool
A user account management log
The primary Linux system logging service recording kernel messages, system events, and application logs
questions.explanation: Syslog is the standard Linux/Unix logging system (daemon: syslogd or rsyslogd) that collects and stores messages from the kernel, system services, and applications in /var/log/syslog or /var/log/messages. Forensically, syslog reveals: service starts/stops, system errors, cron job execution, and security events.
questions.takeaway: Syslog = Linux system log (/var/log/syslog); kernel + service + application events; forensic timeline source

questions.question: What is the difference between packet capture and packet analysis in network forensics?
options: Packet capture records raw network traffic; packet analysis examines captured data to extract intelligence
Packet capture creates reports; packet analysis collects raw data
Packet capture is only done on routers; packet analysis on endpoints
Packet capture requires special hardware; analysis can be done in software
questions.explanation: Packet capture (sniffing) is the process of recording raw network traffic — all frames passing through an interface. Packet analysis is the examination of captured traffic to extract meaningful information: protocol patterns, file transfers, credentials, attack patterns, and timeline reconstruction from the PCAP data.
questions.takeaway: Packet capture = Record raw traffic; Packet analysis = Examine captures to extract intelligence and reconstruct events

questions.question: What is a PCAP file and which tool primarily works with it?
options: A compressed forensic image format
A Packet CAPture file containing recorded network traffic; primarily used with Wireshark/tcpdump
A password capture file from a brute-force attack
A process capture file from Sysinternals
questions.explanation: A PCAP (Packet CAPture) file is the standard format for storing captured network traffic, containing all raw packet data including headers and payloads with timestamps. Wireshark and tcpdump are the primary tools for creating and analyzing PCAP files. PCAPs are critical evidence in network intrusion investigations.
questions.takeaway: PCAP = Packet capture file storing raw network traffic with timestamps; Wireshark/tcpdump for creation and analysis

questions.question: What is the difference between a capture filter and a display filter in Wireshark?
options: Capture filters are applied after capture; display filters are applied during capture
They are identical; just different names for the same feature
Capture filters limit what traffic is recorded; display filters limit what captured traffic is shown
Display filters are faster than capture filters
questions.explanation: Capture filters (using BPF syntax) are applied BEFORE capturing — only matching packets are recorded, reducing file size. Display filters (using Wireshark's own syntax) are applied AFTER capture to show/hide packets without losing data. Example: capture filter 'tcp port 80'; display filter 'http.request.method == "GET"'.
questions.takeaway: Capture filter = Filters BEFORE recording (BPF syntax); Display filter = Filters AFTER capture (Wireshark syntax)

questions.question: What Wireshark display filter shows only HTTP GET requests?
options: http.get == true
http.request == GET
tcp.port == 80 and method == GET
http.request.method == "GET"
questions.explanation: The correct Wireshark display filter for HTTP GET requests is: http.request.method == "GET". Other useful filters: ip.addr == 192.168.1.1 (filter by IP), tcp.port == 443 (filter by port), dns (show only DNS), icmp (show only ICMP), tcp.flags.syn == 1 (SYN packets only).
questions.takeaway: Wireshark GET filter = http.request.method == "GET"; ip.addr, tcp.port, dns, icmp are common display filters

questions.question: What is 'live capture' vs 'PCAP analysis' in network forensics?
options: Live capture monitors real-time network traffic; PCAP analysis examines previously recorded traffic files
They refer to the same process
Live capture uses hardware sniffers; PCAP analysis uses software only
Live capture analyzes historical data; PCAP analysis captures new traffic
questions.explanation: Live capture monitors network traffic in real time on an active network interface, useful for watching ongoing attacks or communications. PCAP analysis examines previously recorded traffic files (.pcap), useful when investigators have captured traffic from an incident and need to reconstruct what happened.
questions.takeaway: Live capture = Real-time monitoring of current traffic; PCAP analysis = Examining previously recorded .pcap files

questions.question: What does a network packet contain at its fundamental structure?
options: Only the payload data
Frame (physical layer), Headers (network/transport layer addressing/control), and Payload (actual data)
Only headers and routing information
Only source and destination IP addresses
questions.explanation: A network packet/frame consists of: Frame (Layer 2 physical addressing — MAC source/destination, EtherType), Headers (Layer 3 IP and Layer 4 TCP/UDP — source/destination IPs, ports, sequence numbers, flags, TTL), and Payload (the actual application data). Each layer adds its own header as data travels down the stack.
questions.takeaway: Packet structure: Frame (L2 MAC) + Headers (L3 IP, L4 TCP/UDP) + Payload (application data)

questions.question: In Wireshark, what does the display filter 'tcp.flags.syn == 1 and tcp.flags.ack == 0' detect?
options: FIN packets indicating connection termination
Established TCP connections
SYN packets (initial connection requests), used to detect port scanning or SYN flood attempts
RST packets indicating connection reset
questions.explanation: tcp.flags.syn == 1 and tcp.flags.ack == 0 isolates pure SYN packets (TCP connection initiation requests without ACK). A burst of these to multiple ports from one source indicates a SYN port scan (nmap -sS). A large volume to one port indicates a SYN flood DoS attack. This filter is essential for network intrusion analysis.
questions.takeaway: SYN-only filter = tcp.flags.syn==1 and tcp.flags.ack==0; detects port scans (multiple ports) and SYN floods

questions.question: What Windows Event ID records a successful user logon?
options: Event ID 4625
Event ID 4648
Event ID 4672
Event ID 4624
questions.explanation: Windows Security Event ID 4624 records a successful account logon. Key related events: 4625 = Failed logon (wrong password), 4648 = Logon using explicit credentials (runas), 4672 = Special privileges assigned (admin logon), 4634 = Logoff. These events are essential for forensic timeline reconstruction.
questions.takeaway: Event ID 4624 = Successful logon; 4625 = Failed logon; 4672 = Admin privileges assigned; 4634 = Logoff

questions.question: What Windows Event ID records a failed logon attempt?
options: Event ID 4625
Event ID 4776
Event ID 4624
Event ID 4720
questions.explanation: Event ID 4625 records a failed account logon attempt. It includes the Sub Status code identifying the reason: 0xC000006A = wrong password, 0xC0000064 = non-existent username, 0xC000006E = account restriction. Multiple 4625 events followed by 4624 may indicate a brute-force attack.
questions.takeaway: Event ID 4625 = Failed logon; Sub Status 0xC000006A = wrong password; pattern = brute force indicator

questions.question: What Windows Event ID records a new user account being created?
options: Event ID 4624
Event ID 4720
Event ID 4648
Event ID 4732
questions.explanation: Event ID 4720 records the creation of a new user account in Windows. Forensically, unexpected account creation events may indicate: attacker persistence (creating backdoor accounts), privilege escalation, or insider threat activity. Related: 4722 = Account enabled, 4723 = Password change attempt, 4726 = Account deleted.
questions.takeaway: Event ID 4720 = User account created; forensically indicates persistence; 4722 = enabled; 4726 = deleted

questions.question: What Windows Event ID records a process being created?
options: Event ID 4663
Event ID 4697
Event ID 4688
Event ID 4656
questions.explanation: Event ID 4688 (Process Creation) records every new process created on the system including the process name, full command line (if audit command line is enabled), creator process, and token information. This is invaluable for detecting malware execution, fileless attacks, and lateral movement commands.
questions.takeaway: Event ID 4688 = Process creation; includes command line; detects malware execution and fileless attacks

questions.question: What Windows Event ID indicates that a user was added to a privileged group?
options: Event ID 4720
Event ID 4728
Event ID 4732
All of the above depending on group
questions.explanation: Group membership changes are logged differently by group type: 4728 = Member added to a security-enabled global group, 4732 = Member added to a security-enabled local group, 4756 = Member added to a universal group. All three may indicate privilege escalation when the group is Administrators or Domain Admins.
questions.takeaway: Group membership: 4728 = global group; 4732 = local group; 4756 = universal group; watch for admin groups

questions.question: What is the significance of Epoch time in digital forensics?
options: Epoch time (Unix timestamp) is seconds since Jan 1, 1970 UTC — a universal timestamp for cross-system correlation
Epoch time is a format only used in Linux file systems
Epoch time cannot be converted to human-readable format
Epoch time is used only in network packet analysis
questions.explanation: Epoch time (Unix timestamp) is the number of seconds since January 1, 1970 00:00:00 UTC. It is used universally across Linux, network protocols, databases, and log files. In forensics, converting epoch timestamps to local time (accounting for time zones) is essential for correlating events across multiple systems.
questions.takeaway: Epoch time = Seconds since 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC; universal timestamp; essential for cross-system event correlation

questions.question: Why must forensic investigators document both UTC time and local time when collecting evidence?
options: Only UTC is needed; local time adds confusion
Because system clocks may be wrong, and local time zones affect timestamp interpretation across jurisdictions
To comply with international forensic standards only
Because UTC is used for Linux and local time for Windows
questions.explanation: Documenting both UTC and local time is essential because: (1) system clocks may be wrong (CMOS clock drift), (2) time zone offsets differ across regions, (3) daylight saving time changes can cause hour-long discrepancies, and (4) evidence spanning multiple time zones requires a common UTC reference for accurate timeline correlation.
questions.takeaway: Document UTC + local time: System clocks may drift, time zones differ, DST causes offsets — UTC is the forensic standard

questions.question: What unique challenges do mobile devices present in digital forensics compared to traditional computers?
options: Mobile devices have no storage that can be analyzed
Mobile forensics requires physical access to carriers only
Mobile devices use proprietary operating systems, may auto-wipe, have cloud sync, and are always connected
Mobile devices use standard NTFS file systems
questions.explanation: Mobile forensics challenges include: proprietary OS (Android/iOS) with different forensic approaches, remote wipe capability (attacker or owner can delete evidence remotely), cloud sync meaning evidence may be distributed across servers, encryption by default on modern devices, and carrier data requiring legal process to obtain.
questions.takeaway: Mobile forensic challenges: Proprietary OS + remote wipe risk + cloud sync + default encryption + carrier data

questions.question: What is the FIRST action an investigator should take when seizing a mobile device as evidence?
options: Immediately attempt to unlock the device to preserve evidence
Turn off the device and place it in a static-proof bag
Remove the SIM card and battery as quickly as possible
Place the device in airplane mode or a Faraday bag to prevent remote wipe and communication
questions.explanation: Immediately isolating a mobile device from all network communications is critical. Place it in airplane mode (if accessible) or — better — in a Faraday cage/bag that blocks all RF signals (cellular, WiFi, Bluetooth). This prevents: remote wipe commands, lock PIN changes, new data being received, and location tracking.
questions.takeaway: First mobile seizure action = Faraday bag or airplane mode; blocks remote wipe and preserves current device state

questions.question: What is a Faraday bag/cage in mobile forensics?
options: A signal-blocking enclosure that prevents any wireless communication to/from a device
A bag with anti-humidity properties for evidence preservation
A static-proof bag for storing hard drives
A specialized transport container for fragile electronic components
questions.explanation: A Faraday cage/bag is an enclosure lined with conductive material (metallic mesh or foil) that blocks electromagnetic signals. In mobile forensics, it prevents the device from receiving or transmitting cellular, WiFi, Bluetooth, and GPS signals — blocking remote wipe commands and preserving the device's current state.
questions.takeaway: Faraday bag = Signal-blocking enclosure; prevents cellular/WiFi/Bluetooth signals; stops remote wipe during seizure

questions.question: What are the four levels of mobile device extraction in order of depth?
options: Quick scan, Full scan, Deep scan, Ultra scan
Manual, Logical, File system, Physical
User, App, System, Kernel
Basic, Intermediate, Advanced, Expert
questions.explanation: Mobile extraction levels (least to most comprehensive): Manual (viewing screen and photographing), Logical (using API to extract allocated files — similar to iTunes backup), File System (accessing the full file system via ADB/jailbreak), Physical (bit-by-bit image of flash memory — most complete, requires bypass).
questions.takeaway: Mobile extraction: Manual < Logical < File System < Physical (most to least complete; physical = bit-by-bit flash image)

questions.question: What CDAC/CCEE exam knowledge area covers mobile forensics evidence artifacts?
options: SMS messages are stored in /data/data/com.android.providers.telephony/databases/mmssms.db
Mobile artifacts cannot be extracted forensically
Both A and B
Call logs are stored in /data/data/com.android.providers.telephony
questions.explanation: On Android devices, call logs and SMS/MMS messages are stored in SQLite databases under /data/data/com.android.providers.telephony/. The mmssms.db file contains SMS/MMS messages; calllog.db contains call history. These require physical/file system extraction as they are in the protected /data partition.
questions.takeaway: Android SMS/calls = SQLite DBs in /data/data/com.android.providers.telephony/; requires physical/file system extraction

questions.question: What is the correct procedure for photographing a computer crime scene?
options: Only photograph the computer equipment, not surroundings
Only photograph the screen contents before touching anything
Photographs are not required if written notes are taken
Photograph the entire scene, including all equipment, cable connections, screen contents, and surrounding area
questions.explanation: Complete crime scene photography includes: overall scene shots, close-ups of each device and its serial number, cable connections (back of devices), screen contents, surrounding environment (post-it notes with passwords, physical security), and any potentially relevant physical items. Photographs provide an unambiguous record.
questions.takeaway: Crime scene photography = Full scene + equipment + cables + screen + surroundings; complete visual documentation

questions.question: What information should be obtained from a computer's BIOS/CMOS during forensic investigation?
options: The system's date and time (for time discrepancy documentation), boot order, and hardware configuration
The complete hard drive contents
The network adapter's MAC address
The user's password for Windows login
questions.explanation: BIOS/CMOS should be checked for: (1) system date and time — document the difference from known good time (UTC) for timestamp calibration, (2) boot order — ensure forensic boot media takes precedence, (3) hardware configuration (drive presence). CMOS time discrepancy must be documented and applied to all timestamps.
questions.takeaway: BIOS/CMOS check = System time (document discrepancy for calibration) + boot order + hardware config

questions.question: What is the purpose of documenting BIOS time discrepancy in a forensic investigation?
options: To demonstrate the suspect tampered with the clock
To calculate the offset between system time and accurate time, adjusting all file timestamps accordingly
Only required for legal purposes, not technical analysis
To prove the computer is old and outdated
questions.explanation: BIOS time discrepancy = the difference between the CMOS clock and actual accurate time (from NTP or reference). This offset must be applied to all file timestamps extracted from the system. If the BIOS clock is 45 minutes ahead, all file timestamps are 45 minutes ahead — investigators must subtract this offset for accurate timeline.
questions.takeaway: BIOS time discrepancy = Offset applied to all file timestamps; critical for accurate forensic timeline reconstruction

questions.question: What is the correct handling procedure for a running computer at a crime scene where the screen shows active encryption?
options: Close all windows to prevent data destruction
Reboot the computer to clear the encryption
Perform live RAM acquisition immediately to capture potential encryption keys before shutdown
Immediately pull the power cord to preserve the encrypted state
questions.explanation: When active encryption is detected on a running system, the priority is live RAM acquisition using tools like WinPmem, DumpIt, or FTK Imager (memory dump) before any shutdown. Encryption keys may reside in RAM and will be irretrievably lost on shutdown. This is the most time-critical forensic action in this scenario.
questions.takeaway: Active encryption = Emergency RAM acquisition first; encryption keys in RAM will be lost on shutdown — capture NOW

questions.question: What is the purpose of creating a 'forensic duplicate' rather than a simple file copy?
options: Forensic duplicates are faster to create than file copies
Forensic duplicates are smaller than file copies
File copies are illegal for forensic purposes
Forensic duplicates capture the complete disk including unallocated space, slack, and deleted data that file copies miss
questions.explanation: A simple file copy only copies allocated files — what the OS sees. A forensic duplicate (bit-stream image) captures EVERYTHING: allocated files, unallocated space (deleted file remnants), file slack space, partial files, and file system structures. Simple copies miss the forensic evidence that makes prosecutions possible.
questions.takeaway: Forensic duplicate vs file copy: Forensic captures unallocated + slack + deleted; file copy only gets allocated files

questions.question: What is accreditation in the context of digital forensic laboratories?
options: Formal recognition by an authoritative body that a lab meets specified competence and quality standards
The process of registering forensic tools with law enforcement
Government approval to handle classified evidence
The licensing process for forensic investigators
questions.explanation: Lab accreditation (e.g., ASCLD/LAB, ISO/IEC 17025) is formal recognition that a forensic lab has demonstrated competence, impartiality, and consistent quality in its testing and calibration activities. Accredited labs are more trusted by courts; their methods and results carry greater legal weight.
questions.takeaway: Lab accreditation (ASCLD, ISO 17025) = Formal recognition of lab competence; increases court credibility of findings

questions.question: How does privacy law affect forensic investigators when examining personal devices?
options: Privacy law only applies to law enforcement, not corporate forensics
Privacy laws (like GDPR, ECPA) may restrict what data can be examined without proper legal authority (warrant/consent)
Privacy law has no effect on forensic investigations
Privacy law prevents forensic examination of encrypted devices
questions.explanation: Privacy laws create constraints: ECPA (Electronic Communications Privacy Act) restricts access to stored and transmitted communications; GDPR limits what data can be collected and processed; the 4th Amendment protects against unreasonable search. Corporate investigators must ensure proper authorization (AUP, employee consent) before accessing personal data.
questions.takeaway: Privacy laws (GDPR, ECPA, 4th Amendment) restrict forensic access; proper legal authority (warrant/consent) required

questions.question: What is the SWGDE (Scientific Working Group for Digital Evidence) and why is it relevant?
options: A government agency that prosecutes cybercrime
A software company that produces forensic tools
An organization that develops standards and best practices for digital evidence handling to ensure legal admissibility
An international law enforcement coordination body
questions.explanation: SWGDE (Scientific Working Group for Digital Evidence) develops and promotes standards, best practices, and guidelines for the acquisition, examination, and analysis of digital evidence. Following SWGDE guidelines helps ensure evidence is collected and analyzed in ways that will be accepted by courts.
questions.takeaway: SWGDE = Standards body for digital evidence best practices; following SWGDE guidelines improves court admissibility

questions.question: What is the ISO/IEC 27037 standard relevant to digital forensics?
options: A standard for information security management systems
A framework for cybersecurity incident classification
A standard for forensic tool certification
Guidelines for identification, collection, acquisition, and preservation of digital evidence
questions.explanation: ISO/IEC 27037 provides international guidelines for the identification, collection, acquisition, and preservation of digital evidence. It establishes principles of auditability, repeatability, justifiability, and minimizing contamination. Following ISO 27037 demonstrates a globally recognized standard of forensic practice to courts.
questions.takeaway: ISO/IEC 27037 = International standard for digital evidence identification, collection, acquisition, preservation

questions.question: Which CDAC/CCEE exam topic covers the concept of 'imaging errors and risks'?
options: Bad sectors on source drive cause imaging to fail or produce incomplete images requiring error handling
Imaging errors are only relevant for RAID arrays
Imaging errors never occur with hardware write blockers
Errors occur when the destination drive is smaller than source
questions.explanation: Imaging errors frequently occur due to bad sectors on the source drive. Tools like dcfldd and dd_rescue handle read errors by: skipping bad sectors (recording their location), retrying reads, and padding with zeros. Complete documentation of imaging errors is essential — courts need to know if any sectors were unreadable.
questions.takeaway: Imaging errors = Bad sectors cause incomplete images; use dd_rescue/dcfldd; document all error locations

questions.question: What happens to evidence integrity if imaging is performed without a write blocker and the OS mounts the evidence drive?
options: The imaging process will automatically compensate for any changes
The OS may modify last-access timestamps and write system files, causing hash mismatch and evidence contamination
The image hash will still match the source hash
Only Windows OS causes contamination; Linux does not
questions.explanation: Without a write blocker, when an OS mounts an evidence drive: Windows updates last-access times for directories, may create System Volume Information, and updates the MFT. Linux may mount and write journal recovery entries. These changes alter the drive, causing the post-mounting hash to differ from the pre-imaging hash — contamination.
questions.takeaway: No write blocker + OS mount = Timestamp modifications + journal writes = hash mismatch = evidence contamination

questions.question: How do courts view evidence contamination in digital forensic cases?
options: Courts only care about contamination in physical evidence, not digital
Minor contamination is acceptable if documented
Courts may rule contaminated evidence inadmissible, potentially causing the entire case to fail
Courts accept contaminated evidence if the investigator is certified
questions.explanation: Courts take evidence contamination extremely seriously. Defense attorneys routinely challenge evidence integrity through expert witnesses. Contaminated digital evidence (modified timestamps, altered files, broken chain of custody) can be ruled inadmissible — even if the contamination was accidental. A single contamination event can invalidate an entire forensic examination.
questions.takeaway: Courts + contamination = Evidence ruled inadmissible; single contamination can invalidate entire forensic examination

questions.question: What is 'live imaging' (live acquisition) vs 'dead imaging'?
options: Live imaging uses network; dead imaging uses physical connection
Dead imaging is illegal in most jurisdictions
Live imaging is done on compromised systems; dead imaging on clean systems
Live imaging acquires data from a running system; dead imaging acquires from a powered-off system
questions.explanation: Live imaging captures data from a running system — including volatile memory (RAM), which is impossible in dead imaging. Dead imaging (traditional forensics) powers off the system and images the drive in a controlled environment. Live imaging is preferred when volatile evidence (RAM, encryption keys, network state) must be preserved.
questions.takeaway: Live imaging = Running system (captures volatile RAM + network state); Dead imaging = Powered off (disk only)

questions.question: What is the AFF (Advanced Forensics Format) and its key feature?
options: An open forensic image format supporting compression, encryption, and extensive embedded metadata
A proprietary format only readable by EnCase
A format used exclusively for mobile device imaging
A format that automatically verifies evidence on acquisition
questions.explanation: AFF (Advanced Forensics Format) is an open-source forensic image format designed to address limitations of DD and E01. Key features: compression (reduces storage), encryption (AES), extensive embedded metadata (case info, investigator, acquisition details), segment authentication (MD5/SHA-1), and no proprietary licensing. Supported by tools like FTK Imager, Sleuth Kit.
questions.takeaway: AFF = Open-source format; compression + encryption + extensive metadata + segment authentication; no proprietary lock-in

questions.question: When is file-level imaging (logical imaging) acceptable in forensic investigations?
options: Never; physical imaging is always required
When the investigation scope is limited to specific files/folders and complete disk acquisition is impractical
Only for mobile device investigations
Always; it is faster and equally valid
questions.explanation: Logical/file-level imaging is acceptable when: (1) the investigation scope is specific (e.g., 'collect all emails'), (2) full disk imaging is impractical due to size (petabyte storage arrays), (3) for live systems where only specific data matters, (4) for remote acquisitions. Document the justification for choosing logical over physical.
questions.takeaway: Logical imaging acceptable when: Specific scope + impractical full imaging + large storage + documented justification

questions.question: What is a hardware write blocker and give an example?
options: A firewall device preventing network access to evidence
A hardware device that encrypts data during imaging
A physical hardware device (e.g., Tableau T35es, WiebeTech) placed between evidence drive and forensic workstation
Software installed on the forensic workstation to prevent writes
questions.explanation: Hardware write blockers (e.g., Tableau T35es SATA/IDE, WiebeTech CRU USB 3.0, Logicube Forensic Dossier) are physical devices with evidence drive on one side and forensic workstation on the other. They electrically intercept and block write commands at the hardware level, providing the strongest evidence protection.
questions.takeaway: Hardware write blockers (Tableau, WiebeTech) = Physical devices blocking writes at hardware level; strongest evidence protection

questions.question: What changes occur on an evidence drive when a computer boots without a write blocker?
options: Changes only occur if the user logs in
Only the MBR is modified during boot
No changes occur if the drive is only observed during boot
Last-access timestamps updated, swap file modified, temp files created, registry accessed, journal entries written
questions.explanation: Without write blocker, booting a computer modifies the evidence drive: (1) Directory last-access timestamps updated, (2) Windows pagefile.sys/swapfile.sys modified, (3) Prefetch files created/updated, (4) Registry hives loaded and modified (last write times), (5) Event logs written, (6) System Restore points potentially created, (7) NTFS journal entries written.
questions.takeaway: Boot without write blocker: Timestamps, pagefile, prefetch, registry, event logs, restore points, NTFS journal — ALL modified

questions.question: What is the boot sequence in a BIOS-based system and its forensic relevance?
options: POST → BIOS → MBR → Boot loader → OS kernel; forensic boot media must be first in boot order
BIOS only loads the OS without a defined sequence
MBR → BIOS → POST → OS → Boot loader
OS loads first, then BIOS configures hardware
questions.explanation: BIOS boot sequence: Power → POST (hardware check) → BIOS reads boot order → reads MBR from first boot device → MBR calls bootloader (GRUB/NTLDR) → bootloader loads OS kernel. Forensically, investigators set USB/CD as first in boot order to boot forensic OS (SIFT, Kali) without touching the evidence drive.
questions.takeaway: BIOS boot: POST → BIOS → MBR → Bootloader → OS; set forensic USB first in boot order to avoid evidence drive

questions.question: What is UEFI and how does it differ from BIOS in a forensic context?
options: UEFI is an older version of BIOS with fewer features
UEFI is a modern firmware interface with Secure Boot that may prevent booting from unsigned forensic USB drives
UEFI only affects server systems, not forensic workstations
UEFI and BIOS are identical in forensic impact
questions.explanation: UEFI (Unified Extensible Firmware Interface) replaces BIOS with faster boot, GPT disk support, and Secure Boot. Secure Boot cryptographically verifies bootloaders — only signed code executes. Forensically, Secure Boot may prevent booting standard forensic Linux distributions (SIFT, Kali) unless Secure Boot is disabled or the forensic tool is signed.
questions.takeaway: UEFI Secure Boot = May block forensic boot drives; disable Secure Boot in UEFI settings to boot forensic OS

questions.question: What is the USN Journal ($UsnJrnl) in NTFS and its forensic value?
options: A user activity log recording keyboard input
A backup of the MFT created during system shutdown
An NTFS transaction journal recording all file system changes (create, modify, delete, rename) with timestamps
A log file created by Windows Update
questions.explanation: The USN Journal ($UsnJrnl) is an NTFS metadata file in $Extend that records a chronological log of all file system changes: file creation, modification, deletion, renaming, attribute changes, and security descriptor changes, all with timestamps. Even after file deletion, the USN Journal may retain records of the deleted file's activity.
questions.takeaway: USN Journal = NTFS log of all file changes with timestamps; records deleted file activity; survived deletion

questions.question: What are Windows Prefetch files and their forensic significance?
options: System recovery files stored in %SystemRoot%\Prefetch
Browser cache files stored for offline viewing
Temporary files created by applications during installation
Files created by Windows to speed up program loading; forensically reveal program execution history with timestamps
questions.explanation: Windows Prefetch files (.pf) in %SystemRoot%\Prefetch are created when programs execute, recording: executable name and path, last 8 run times (Windows 8+), run count, and files/directories accessed during execution. Forensically, they prove a program was executed even if the program itself was deleted — critical for malware investigation.
questions.takeaway: Prefetch files = Program execution history (name, path, run count, last 8 timestamps); proves execution even after deletion

questions.question: What is the Windows $Recycle.Bin and its forensic artifacts?
options: Stores deleted files with metadata: original path, deletion timestamp, and original filename in separate metadata files
A system folder with no forensic value
A cache folder for deleted Windows updates only
A folder containing backup copies of all deleted files
questions.explanation: The Windows $Recycle.Bin folder stores deleted files before permanent deletion. Each deleted file has two forensic artifacts: the $R file (contains actual deleted file data) and the $I file (metadata: original file path, original filename, deletion date/time, original file size). Even if $R is overwritten, $I metadata persists.
questions.takeaway: $Recycle.Bin forensics: $R file = deleted data; $I file = original path + filename + deletion timestamp + size

questions.question: What is LNK file analysis in Windows forensics?
options: Examination of network link state advertisements
Examination of Windows shortcut (.lnk) files that record recently accessed files and their original paths
Analysis of symbolic links in NTFS
Analysis of Linux network kernel configuration files
questions.explanation: Windows LNK files (shell link files / .lnk shortcuts) are created automatically when files are accessed. They store: the original file's full path, volume serial number, MAC address of the machine, file size, creation/modification/access timestamps of the target. LNK files persist even after the original file is deleted — proving file access.
questions.takeaway: LNK files = Auto-created shortcuts recording accessed file paths, MAC address, timestamps; persist after original deletion

questions.question: What is the Windows Shellbags forensic artifact?
options: Bags of evidence stored in Windows System folders
Browser download history stored in the shell namespace
Registry keys recording folder navigation history including previously accessed directories and display settings
Temporary files created by Windows Explorer
questions.explanation: Shellbags are Registry entries (in NTUSER.DAT and USRCLASS.DAT) that Windows Explorer creates to remember folder view settings (icon size, sort order). Forensically they prove folder access — including network shares, USB drives, and zip files — even after the folders no longer exist. They persist long after the accessed drive is removed.
questions.takeaway: Shellbags = Registry records of folder access (view settings); prove USB/network folder access even after device removal

questions.question: What is the Amcache.hve file in Windows forensics?
options: An encrypted backup of the SAM database
A hash value cache for Windows Update files
A cache file for Windows Activation
A registry hive recording recently installed programs and executed applications with SHA-1 hashes
questions.explanation: Amcache.hve (located in %SystemRoot%\AppCompat\Programs\) is a Registry hive that records information about installed and executed programs including: full file path, executable SHA-1 hash, installation/execution timestamps, and publisher information. It can identify malware that was executed even if deleted, since the SHA-1 hash persists.
questions.takeaway: Amcache.hve = Program execution + install history with SHA-1 hashes; identifies deleted malware by hash

questions.question: What is the Windows Shimcache (AppCompatCache) artifact?
options: A registry artifact recording recently executed programs for OS compatibility checking, including timestamps
A Windows compatibility layer for old software
A log of Windows Update compatibility checks
A cache of browser compatibility settings
questions.explanation: Shimcache (Application Compatibility Cache) stored in HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\AppCompatCache records every executable that Windows processes for compatibility checking, including file path, size, last modification time, and execution flag. It proves file presence and potentially execution.
questions.takeaway: Shimcache = HKLM SYSTEM hive; records executables processed for compatibility; proves file presence and execution

questions.question: What is a forensic timeline and how is it created?
options: A legal timeline showing the evidence chain of custody
A chronological sequence of all file system and log events (using timestamps from multiple sources) to reconstruct an attack
A timeline showing when forensic tools were used during an investigation
A chronological list of evidence items by collection date
questions.explanation: A forensic timeline combines timestamps from multiple sources: file system timestamps (MACE), Windows Event Logs, Registry timestamps (last write times), browser history, email headers, network logs, and application logs. Tools like Autopsy, log2timeline/Plaso, and Sleuth Kit's mactime create unified timelines for attack reconstruction.
questions.takeaway: Forensic timeline = Timestamps from file system + event logs + registry + browser + network; reconstructs attack sequence

questions.question: What is the Sleuth Kit and Autopsy in digital forensics?
options: Government-developed forensic tools restricted to law enforcement
Commercial forensic tools developed by EnCase
Open-source forensic frameworks: Sleuth Kit provides command-line disk analysis; Autopsy is its graphical front-end
Hardware tools for drive imaging
questions.explanation: The Sleuth Kit (TSK) is an open-source collection of command-line tools for file system and disk analysis (mmls, fls, icat, mactime). Autopsy is a free open-source graphical digital forensics platform that provides a GUI front-end for The Sleuth Kit and adds features like timeline analysis, keyword search, and hash lookups.
questions.takeaway: Sleuth Kit = Open-source CLI disk analysis tools; Autopsy = Free GUI forensic platform built on Sleuth Kit

questions.question: What is RAM acquisition and which tools are used for it?
options: Downloading additional RAM capacity from the cloud
Compressing RAM data to reduce acquisition time
Analyzing previously saved RAM screenshots
Capturing the complete contents of a running computer's physical memory for volatile evidence preservation
questions.explanation: RAM acquisition captures a bit-for-bit image of a computer's physical memory while the system is running. Tools: WinPmem (free, Windows), DumpIt (simple Windows memory dump), Magnet RAM Capture (free), Volatility's memory acquisition modules, and FTK Imager (memory acquisition feature). RAM images reveal encryption keys, passwords, running malware, and network connections.
questions.takeaway: RAM acquisition tools: WinPmem, DumpIt, Magnet RAM Capture, FTK Imager; captures encryption keys, passwords, malware

questions.question: What is the Volatility framework used for in digital forensics?
options: Memory forensics — analyzing RAM dumps to extract processes, network connections, encryption keys, and malware
Disk image analysis and file recovery
Network packet capture and analysis
Log file analysis and event correlation
questions.explanation: Volatility is the leading open-source memory forensics framework for analyzing RAM dumps. Key capabilities: list running processes (pslist, pstree), extract network connections (netscan), find injected code (malfind), extract registry hives (hivelist/printkey), dump process memory (memdump), and identify rootkits (ssdt). Supports Windows, Linux, and macOS memory images.
questions.takeaway: Volatility = Open-source memory forensics; analyzes RAM dumps for processes, network, injected code, registry, rootkits

questions.question: What does the Volatility command 'volatility -f mem.raw pslist' do?
options: Shows all network connections in the RAM dump
Lists all running processes from the RAM dump with PID, PPID, start time, and exit time
Extracts the Windows Registry from the RAM dump
Lists all files on disk in the memory image
questions.explanation: The 'pslist' Volatility plugin walks the Windows process list structure (EPROCESS) in memory and lists all active processes with: PID, parent PID (PPID), process name, start time, exit time, and thread/handle count. Malware may hide from pslist using DKOM (Direct Kernel Object Manipulation) — use psscan as complement.
questions.takeaway: Volatility pslist = Lists active processes from RAM; DKOM malware may hide; use psscan to detect hidden processes

questions.question: What is DKOM (Direct Kernel Object Manipulation) in malware analysis?
options: A technique for manipulating Windows Registry keys at kernel level
A method for directly accessing disk sectors bypassing the OS
A rootkit technique unlinking malicious processes from kernel lists to hide from process listing tools
A kernel debugging technique used by forensic investigators
questions.explanation: DKOM is an advanced rootkit technique that manipulates kernel data structures (specifically the EPROCESS doubly-linked list) to unlink a malicious process from the list that task managers and pslist query. The process continues running but becomes invisible to standard tools. Volatility's psscan counters DKOM by scanning raw memory for EPROCESS structures.
questions.takeaway: DKOM = Rootkit technique unlinking process from kernel list; hidden from pslist; detected by Volatility's psscan

questions.question: Which of the following is a valid reason to perform a live forensic acquisition instead of a dead box acquisition?
options: Live acquisition requires fewer tools
Live acquisition is always faster
Dead box acquisition cannot handle large drives
The system uses full-disk encryption and the encryption keys may be in RAM
questions.explanation: Full-disk encryption (BitLocker, VeraCrypt, LUKS) is the primary reason to choose live acquisition — the encryption keys must be extracted from RAM before shutdown, otherwise the drive content will be inaccessible. Other reasons: highly volatile network state needed, server that cannot be taken offline, active attacker session to observe.
questions.takeaway: Live acquisition when full-disk encryption is active = Capture keys from RAM before shutdown makes disk unreadable

questions.question: What is the 'order of operations' investigators must follow to maximize forensic evidence preservation?
options: Document scene → Capture volatile data (RAM, network state) → Isolate system → Disk imaging
Image disk first → Then RAM → Then network state → Document last
Disconnect network → RAM acquisition → Disk imaging → Documentation
Power off immediately → Document → Image disk → Analyze
questions.explanation: Optimal evidence preservation order: Document scene (photograph) → Network isolation (Faraday/unplug) → Capture volatile data in order of volatility (network state, RAM, processes) → Create forensic disk image (with write blocker) → Log all actions. Documentation and volatile capture must precede shutdown to avoid evidence loss.
questions.takeaway: Preservation order: Document → Isolate → Volatile data (RAM/network) → Disk image → Log everything

questions.question: What tool in the Sysinternals Suite is specifically used to analyze strings and readable text in files and memory?
options: Handle
Strings
Listdlls
Autoruns
questions.explanation: Strings (Sysinternals) scans executable files, DLLs, and memory dumps for readable ASCII and Unicode strings. In malware analysis, strings reveals: hardcoded IP addresses, URLs, registry keys, file paths, API function names, and error messages embedded in executables — providing intelligence without executing the malware.
questions.takeaway: Strings (Sysinternals) = Extracts readable ASCII/Unicode from executables; reveals malware URLs, IPs, registry keys

questions.question: What is the key forensic difference between NTFS and FAT32 regarding deleted file recovery?
options: NTFS makes deleted file recovery impossible; FAT32 makes it easy
Both file systems handle deletion identically
NTFS retains more metadata (MFT entries with full timestamps) after deletion; FAT32 loses cluster linkage but may retain data
FAT32 retains all metadata after deletion; NTFS does not
questions.explanation: In NTFS, deleting a file marks the MFT entry as available but retains all metadata (timestamps, filename, cluster pointers) until the MFT entry is reused. In FAT32, deletion zeroes the first character of the directory entry and marks FAT clusters as available — losing cluster linkage but retaining data until overwritten. NTFS provides richer forensic artifacts post-deletion.
questions.takeaway: NTFS deletion: MFT entry retained with full metadata; FAT32 deletion: directory entry zeroed, FAT chain lost

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'mactime' tool in The Sleuth Kit forensics framework?
options: Displays the current system time on macOS
Synchronizes time across forensic workstations
Converts timestamps between time zones
Generates a chronological timeline of file system activity using MACE timestamps from a disk image
questions.explanation: mactime (from The Sleuth Kit) generates a body file format then creates a sorted timeline of all file system activity based on MACE timestamps (Modified, Accessed, Changed, Entry Modified). The timeline helps investigators reconstruct what happened chronologically on a system, correlating file creations, modifications, and deletions with known events.
questions.takeaway: mactime (Sleuth Kit) = Generates chronological file system activity timeline from MACE timestamps; attack reconstruction

questions.question: In network forensics, what is the significance of the DNS query log?
options: DNS query logs reveal which domain names were resolved, indicating websites visited and potential C2 communications
DNS logs only record failed name resolution attempts
DNS logs only show configuration errors
DNS logs are not used in forensic investigations
questions.explanation: DNS query logs are highly forensically valuable: they record all hostname lookups including: websites visited (even with HTTPS, the domain is visible), malware C2 domain lookups (even if the IP changes via fast flux), internal network reconnaissance, and data exfiltration over DNS. DNS is often overlooked as an attack vector and forensic source.
questions.takeaway: DNS logs = Domains resolved (websites + C2 lookups); HTTPS visible domain; malware fast-flux detection

questions.question: What is fast flux in the context of malware C2 infrastructure and how does DNS analysis detect it?
options: Fast flux refers to high-volume malware traffic patterns
Fast flux rapidly rotates IP addresses for a C2 domain; DNS analysis detects it by observing very low TTL values and rapidly changing A records
Fast flux is a network attack that floods DNS servers
Fast flux is a technique to speed up DNS resolution
questions.explanation: Fast flux is a DNS evasion technique where a malware C2 domain resolves to rapidly rotating IP addresses (sometimes hundreds) with very low TTL values (seconds to minutes). This makes the C2 infrastructure resilient (no single IP to block) and hard to take down. DNS forensic analysis detects it by observing abnormally low TTLs and large IP rotation patterns.
questions.takeaway: Fast flux = C2 domain rotates IPs rapidly; very low DNS TTL; DNS analysis detects abnormal IP rotation patterns

questions.question: What is the purpose of an incident response 'lessons learned' meeting?
options: To calculate the financial cost of the incident
To assign blame to individuals responsible for the incident
To document what happened, what worked, what failed, and how to improve future response
To present the findings to law enforcement
questions.explanation: The post-incident 'lessons learned' meeting (post-mortem) is the final phase of the NIST IR lifecycle. It documents: incident timeline, detection method, containment effectiveness, what worked, what failed, gaps in security controls, and specific improvements to the IR plan, security architecture, and procedures for future incidents.
questions.takeaway: Lessons learned = Document what happened + what worked/failed + specific improvements; closes NIST IR lifecycle

questions.question: What is the difference between an 'event' and a 'log entry' in digital forensics?
options: Events are physical; log entries are digital
Log entries occur before events; events are the consequences
They are the same thing with different names
An event is something that occurs in a system; a log entry is the recorded documentation of that event
questions.explanation: An event is any occurrence in a system that is potentially significant (user login, file access, service start, error). A log entry is the structured record written to a log file documenting that event — including timestamp, event type, source, severity, and relevant details. Not all events generate log entries depending on audit configuration.
questions.takeaway: Event = Something that occurs in a system; Log entry = Written record documenting that event with timestamp and details

questions.question: What is the 'parent-child' process relationship and why is it forensically significant?
options: Child processes are created by parent processes; unusual parent-child relationships indicate malware or exploitation
Parent-child relationships only matter in Windows, not Linux
Parent processes consume more memory than child processes
Parent processes run in kernel mode; child processes in user mode
questions.explanation: Every process has a parent process that created it (via CreateProcess/fork). Forensically, unusual parent-child relationships indicate compromise: cmd.exe or powershell.exe spawned by a browser (web exploit), svchost.exe with an unexpected parent, or any process launching unexpected children. Malware often injects into legitimate parent processes.
questions.takeaway: Parent-child forensics: Unusual relationships (browser spawning cmd.exe) = Exploitation; normal hierarchy = expected behavior

questions.question: What does PID and PPID stand for, and how are they used in forensic process analysis?
options: Process Identity Device; Primary Process Identity Device
Process ID and Parent Process ID; used to map the complete process tree and identify suspicious spawn chains
Process Index Directory and Previous Process Identifier
Program Identifier and Program Parent Identifier
questions.explanation: PID (Process ID) is a unique number assigned to each running process. PPID (Parent Process ID) identifies which process created it. Building a process tree using PID/PPID relationships reveals the spawn chain: SystemD/init (PID 1) → sshd → bash → suspicious_command. Forensically, broken or unexpected chains indicate malware injection.
questions.takeaway: PID = Process unique ID; PPID = Parent process ID; process tree reveals spawn chains and malware injection points

questions.question: What does 'user mode' vs 'kernel mode' mean in operating system forensics?
options: User mode = GUI applications; Kernel mode = command line applications
They are identical but serve different users
User mode = restricted access for normal applications; Kernel mode = full hardware access for OS kernel and drivers
User mode = read-only; Kernel mode = read-write
questions.explanation: Kernel mode (Ring 0) gives code unrestricted access to hardware and all memory — OS kernel, device drivers, and hardware abstraction layer run here. User mode (Ring 3) restricts application access to only its own memory and must use system calls to access hardware/OS services. Rootkits operate in kernel mode to gain maximum control and hide from user-mode tools.
questions.takeaway: Kernel mode (Ring 0) = Unrestricted hardware access; User mode (Ring 3) = Restricted; rootkits use kernel mode to hide

questions.question: What is thread injection in the context of malware analysis?
options: Injecting network packets into an existing TCP stream
Creating multiple threads to speed up malware execution
A technique for bypassing antivirus scanners during download
Injecting malicious code into the memory space of a legitimate running process to execute under its identity
questions.explanation: Thread injection (process injection) is a malware technique that injects malicious code into the address space of a running legitimate process (e.g., explorer.exe, svchost.exe) and creates a remote thread to execute it. The malicious code runs under the legitimate process's security context and identity, hiding malware activity from process listings.
questions.takeaway: Thread injection = Malicious code injected into legitimate process; executes under trusted identity; detected by malfind in Volatility

questions.question: What is a hash set in forensic tool FTK and how does it improve investigation efficiency?
options: A database of known file hashes used to automatically categorize files as known-good or known-bad
A collection of hash values from the investigator's notes
A collection of hash algorithms used for verification
A set of hash functions used to encrypt evidence files
questions.explanation: A hash set is a database of file hash values used in FTK and other forensic tools to automatically categorize files. Investigators import: NSRL (known-good OS/commercial files — to ignore), known-malware hash sets, or custom sets of known-relevant files. Automatic categorization lets investigators focus on truly relevant/unknown files, dramatically improving efficiency.
questions.takeaway: Hash sets in FTK = Auto-categorize files as known-good (NSRL), known-bad (malware), or unknown; focus investigation

questions.question: What is the significance of the 'creation' timestamp in an NTFS file for forensic investigation?
options: The creation timestamp always accurately reflects when a file was first made
The creation timestamp reflects when the file was copied to the current volume, not necessarily when it was originally created
The creation timestamp is only set by the user explicitly
Creation timestamps cannot be forensically used as they are always inaccurate
questions.explanation: NTFS creation timestamps record when the file was created ON THAT VOLUME. When a file is copied, the destination gets a new creation timestamp (time of copy). When moved within the same volume, the original timestamps are preserved. This means a file's 'creation' time may post-date its 'modified' time — a key indicator it was copied from elsewhere.
questions.takeaway: NTFS created = When file appeared on THIS volume (copy time); Modified = Last content change; Created > Modified = copied file

questions.question: What is the forensic significance of Alternate Data Streams (ADS) in NTFS?
options: ADS are only created by antivirus software during scanning
ADS are backup copies of files automatically created by Windows
ADS are additional data streams within an NTFS file that can hide files/data without visible size change to the main file
ADS are used to store file thumbnails and preview data
questions.explanation: NTFS Alternate Data Streams (ADS) allow multiple data streams to be attached to a single file. Malware can hide entire executables in ADS without changing the visible file size: 'type malware.exe > innocent.txt:hidden.exe'. Standard directory listings and file properties don't reveal ADS. Tools like streams (Sysinternals) detect hidden ADS.
questions.takeaway: NTFS ADS = Hidden data/executables attached to files; invisible to standard dir; detected by Sysinternals Streams tool

questions.question: What is the forensic value of the Windows $MFT file?
options: The MFT contains only currently active file records
The MFT only records system files, not user files
The MFT is a backup copy of the disk's partition table
The MFT contains records for all files ever on the volume; deleted file records persist until overwritten by new files
questions.explanation: The $MFT (Master File Table) contains records for every file and directory on an NTFS volume. When files are deleted, their MFT records are marked as available but not immediately overwritten. Forensic tools parse the $MFT directly from disk to recover complete file metadata (names, timestamps, sizes, cluster locations) for both active and recently deleted files.
questions.takeaway: $MFT forensics = Records for all files (active + recently deleted); parse directly for timestamps, names, paths, clusters

questions.question: What does 'forensic soundness' require when using imaging software like FTK Imager?
options: FTK Imager must verify hash values before and after imaging to confirm no modification occurred
The investigator must be certified in FTK
Images must be stored on RAID arrays for redundancy
FTK Imager must compress all images to save storage
questions.explanation: FTK Imager ensures forensic soundness by: computing hash (MD5 and/or SHA-1) of the source before imaging, computing hash of the resulting image after completion, comparing both hashes, and reporting match/mismatch. A successful hash comparison proves the image is a perfect, unmodified copy of the original evidence.
questions.takeaway: FTK Imager forensic soundness = Pre and post hash computation; matching hashes = verified perfect copy

questions.question: In a CCEE exam context, which command is used to create a raw forensic image on Linux?
options: cat /dev/sda > /mnt/evidence.img
dd if=/dev/sda of=/mnt/evidence.dd bs=4096 conv=noerror,sync
cp /dev/sda /mnt/evidence.img
rsync /dev/sda /mnt/evidence.img
questions.explanation: The correct forensic imaging command: 'dd if=/dev/sda of=/mnt/evidence.dd bs=4096 conv=noerror,sync'. Parameters: if= input file (source drive), of= output file (image destination), bs= block size for efficiency, conv=noerror (continue on read errors), conv=sync (pad unreadable sectors with zeros to maintain offset consistency).
questions.takeaway: dd forensic command: if=source of=output bs=blocksize conv=noerror,sync; noerror=continue; sync=pad bad sectors with zeros

questions.question: What is dcfldd and how does it improve on dd for forensic acquisition?
options: dcfldd is a commercial product requiring licensing
dcfldd is slower than dd but more accurate
dcfldd adds on-the-fly hashing, progress monitoring, and wiping capabilities to the standard dd command
dcfldd is the Windows version of dd
questions.explanation: dcfldd (DC3/DoD dd) is an enhanced version of dd developed by the DoD Computer Forensics Lab. Improvements over dd: on-the-fly MD5/SHA hashing during acquisition (no need for separate hash step), progress reporting (percentage complete), split output files, and pattern-filling capabilities for wiping. All without requiring separate hash computation afterward.
questions.takeaway: dcfldd = Enhanced dd with on-the-fly hashing + progress monitoring + split output; DoD forensic standard

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'sync' option in the forensic dd command 'conv=noerror,sync'?
options: Sync synchronizes the source and destination clocks
Sync forces real-time writing to prevent buffer overflow
Sync enables synchronous I/O for faster imaging
Sync pads unreadable (bad) sectors with zeros to maintain correct data offsets in the image
questions.explanation: The conv=sync option in dd makes each failed read block padded with null bytes (zeros) to fill the full block size. Without sync, dd skips bad sectors and the resulting image has incorrect offsets for all subsequent data. With sync, the image maintains correct spatial alignment — other files remain at their correct positions despite unreadable sectors.
questions.takeaway: conv=sync = Pads bad sectors with zeros; maintains correct data offsets; without sync, image offsets become misaligned

questions.question: What is a 'sparse acquisition' and when would a forensic investigator use it?
options: An acquisition capturing only non-zero allocated data, used when full imaging of very large arrays is impractical
A selective logical acquisition based on file dates only
A quick scan that only checks the first 10% of a drive
An acquisition performed without a write blocker (emergency situation)
questions.explanation: Sparse acquisition copies only the non-zero allocated data blocks from a source, skipping empty/zero-filled blocks. For very large storage (30TB SANs, server RAID arrays), sparse acquisition dramatically reduces image size and acquisition time. It is less forensically complete (misses data in 'zero' blocks), but is necessary for large-scale investigations.
questions.takeaway: Sparse acquisition = Skips zero blocks; smaller image; used for multi-TB arrays; less complete than full physical imaging

questions.question: What is the purpose of a forensic workstation (FRED) and its key specifications?
options: A cloud-based virtual machine for forensic analysis
A dedicated, powerful, pre-configured system with multiple write blocker bays, large storage, and forensic software for reliable evidence processing
A laptop specifically designed for field investigations
Any computer can serve as a forensic workstation
questions.explanation: A FRED (Forensic Recovery of Evidence Device) is a purpose-built forensic workstation with: multiple hardware write blocker connections (IDE, SATA, USB, SCSI), large fast storage for evidence files, validated forensic software (FTK, EnCase), clean/wiped evidence destination drives, and a documented hardware/software configuration for court validation.
questions.takeaway: FRED = Dedicated forensic workstation; multiple write blocker ports, large storage, validated software, court-ready configuration

questions.question: What is the purpose of the NIST Computer Forensics Tool Testing (CFTT) program?
options: To develop new forensic investigation standards
To certify forensic laboratories
To independently test and validate forensic tools to ensure they work as claimed and produce reliable evidence
To train forensic investigators
questions.explanation: NIST CFTT (Computer Forensics Tool Testing) is a program that independently tests forensic hardware and software tools (write blockers, imaging tools, string search tools) against published requirements to verify they work as claimed. CFTT validation reports are used in court to establish that the tools used are reliable and produce accurate results.
questions.takeaway: NIST CFTT = Independent validation of forensic tools; courts use CFTT reports to establish tool reliability

questions.question: What is the difference between evidence 'authentication' and 'integrity verification'?
options: Authentication is for digital evidence; integrity verification for physical evidence
Authentication uses MD5; integrity verification uses SHA
They are the same concept
Authentication proves evidence came from where claimed; integrity verification proves evidence is unaltered since collection
questions.explanation: Authentication establishes evidence genuineness — proving the digital artifact actually came from the claimed source (e.g., the evidence drive from the suspect's computer). Integrity verification proves the evidence has not been altered since collection — typically done with hash comparison. Both are required for evidence admissibility.
questions.takeaway: Authentication = Evidence came from claimed source; Integrity = Evidence unaltered since collection; BOTH required for court

questions.question: What is the role of the 'initial assessment' in a digital forensics investigation?
options: It is the preliminary evaluation of the incident scope, evidence locations, legal authority, and resources needed before investigation begins
It is the final review before submitting evidence to court
It is the assessment of initial suspect motives
It is the first test of forensic tools at the beginning of each investigation
questions.explanation: The initial assessment (preliminary investigation) determines: the nature and scope of the incident, what legal authority exists (warrant, consent, corporate policy), what systems and data are involved, what forensic resources are needed (tools, personnel), jurisdictional issues, and whether evidence may be volatile or at risk. This planning ensures a systematic, legally defensible investigation.
questions.takeaway: Initial assessment = Scope + legal authority + systems involved + resources needed + jurisdiction; before any evidence handling

questions.question: What does the ISO/IEC 27001 standard address and how does it relate to forensics?
options: International standards for cybercrime prosecution
Information Security Management System (ISMS) framework; provides security controls that reduce incidents requiring forensics
Forensic tool certification standards
Digital signature standards for forensic reports
questions.explanation: ISO/IEC 27001 is the international standard for Information Security Management Systems (ISMS). It provides a framework of controls (including incident management, access control, logging, and business continuity) that when implemented, reduce the frequency and impact of security incidents. Forensically, ISO 27001-compliant organizations generate better logs and have clearer incident response procedures.
questions.takeaway: ISO 27001 = ISMS framework; proper logging + incident response procedures improve forensic investigation quality

questions.question: What is the C-DAC CCEE (Centre for Development of Advanced Computing Common Entrance Exam) relevance to cyber forensics?
options: CCEE only tests programming skills
CCEE has no forensics questions
CCEE tests knowledge of computer forensics, cyber security, networking, and OS administration for PG diploma admission
CCEE is only for hardware engineering admissions
questions.explanation: C-DAC CCEE (CDAC Common Entrance Exam) tests candidates for admission to C-DAC's postgraduate programs including PG Diploma in IT Infrastructure, Systems, and Security (PGCP-ITISS). Cyber forensics topics including evidence handling, forensic tools, file systems, and digital investigation procedures are specifically tested.
questions.takeaway: CCEE = C-DAC admission exam; tests cyber forensics, network security, OS administration for PGCP/PGDM programs

questions.question: In CCEE context, what does PGCP-ITISS stand for?
options: Post Graduate Certificate Program - Information Technology Infrastructure and Secure Systems
Post Graduate Certification Program - Internet Technology Systems Security
Professional Graduate Course Program - Information Technology Infrastructure Security
Post Graduate Certificate Program - IT Infrastructure, Systems and Security
questions.explanation: PGCP-ITISS stands for Post Graduate Certificate Program in IT Infrastructure, Systems and Security — a C-DAC program covering cybersecurity, digital forensics, networking, cloud computing, and system administration. Cyber Forensics is a core module including all topics in this question bank.
questions.takeaway: PGCP-ITISS = Post Graduate Certificate in IT Infrastructure, Systems and Security; C-DAC program; forensics is core module

questions.question: Which forensic technique would you use to find hidden files in a Windows system that might contain evidence?
options: Use Streams (Sysinternals) for ADS, Autoruns for startup hidden items, and dir /a for system/hidden attributes
Use Windows search for all files
Run Windows Defender scan to detect hidden files
Check only the Documents folder for hidden files
questions.explanation: Comprehensive hidden file discovery in Windows: 'dir /a' shows files with hidden/system attributes; Sysinternals Streams reveals Alternate Data Streams (ADS) containing hidden data; Autoruns discovers hidden autostart malware; FTK/Autopsy searches for files with hidden attribute or filesystem discrepancies between directory listing and actual disk allocation.
questions.takeaway: Hidden file discovery: dir /a (attributes) + Streams (ADS) + Autoruns (startup) + FTK (filesystem discrepancies)

questions.question: What is the IOCE (International Organization on Computer Evidence) and its forensics principles?
options: A certification body for forensic investigators
An international body establishing principles for digital evidence handling that are accepted globally
A tool for computer evidence collection
A law enforcement agency specializing in cybercrime
questions.explanation: IOCE established internationally accepted principles for digital evidence: (1) Law enforcement must apply general evidence principles, (2) Capturing digital evidence should not change evidence, (3) Trained personnel should handle evidence, (4) All evidence handling must be documented, (5) Evidence custodians are responsible for preservation. These principles underpin all forensic best practices.
questions.takeaway: IOCE principles: Don't change evidence + Trained personnel + Document everything + Custodian responsibility

questions.question: What is the EnCase Evidence Format (E01) segment size and why does segmentation matter?
options: Segmentation is only used for network transfer
Fixed at 1GB; segmentation prevents loss of entire image if one file corrupts
Configurable (default varies); large images are split into multiple E01 segments for file system compatibility and storage
Fixed at 650MB to fit on CDs; a legacy constraint
questions.explanation: E01 images are split into numbered segments (E01, E02, E03...) of configurable size. Segmentation: (1) overcomes FAT32's 4GB file size limit, (2) allows images to span multiple storage devices, (3) each segment has its own CRC32 integrity check, (4) enables parallel processing. Default segment sizes vary by tool (640MB, 2GB, or custom).
questions.takeaway: E01 segmentation = Overcomes FAT32 4GB limit + multiple device storage + per-segment CRC + parallel processing

questions.question: What is the 'triage' concept in digital forensics?
options: The final review of all forensic findings before reporting
Sorting evidence by alphabetical order
A method of separating digital from physical evidence
Rapid preliminary assessment of a device to determine forensic value and prioritize collection in time-limited scenarios
questions.explanation: Forensic triage is rapid preliminary examination of a device (often in the field) to quickly determine forensic relevance and priority. Triage tools (like Magnet AXIOM Triage, COFEE) can scan a live system in minutes to identify: recently accessed files, user accounts, browsing history, and communication patterns — guiding full investigation priority.
questions.takeaway: Forensic triage = Rapid field assessment of forensic value; prioritizes investigation without full imaging; uses quick scan tools

questions.question: What does the term 'anti-forensics' encompass?
options: Techniques used by attackers to frustrate forensic investigation: data hiding, deletion, encryption, and obfuscation
Legal challenges to forensic evidence admissibility
Techniques used by investigators to counter forensic challenges
The study of forensic tool limitations
questions.explanation: Anti-forensics encompasses all techniques used to frustrate digital forensic investigation: secure deletion (multiple overwrites), steganography (hiding data), encryption (making data unreadable), timestomping (altering timestamps), log clearing, rootkit use (hiding processes/files), data obfuscation, and evidence destruction. Each anti-forensic technique has corresponding forensic countermeasures.
questions.takeaway: Anti-forensics = Techniques to frustrate investigation: secure deletion + steganography + encryption + timestomping + log clearing

questions.question: What is secure deletion and how does it differ from normal deletion?
options: Secure deletion requires special hardware; normal deletion uses software
Secure deletion overwrites data multiple times making it forensically unrecoverable; normal deletion only removes references
Secure deletion moves files to an encrypted folder; normal deletion to Recycle Bin
They are the same; both permanently destroy data
questions.explanation: Normal deletion removes directory entries and file system references but leaves data intact until overwritten. Secure deletion tools (Eraser, shred, DBAN) overwrite the actual data sectors multiple times with patterns (DoD 5220.22-M standard: 3-pass, Gutmann: 35-pass) making recovery impractical even with advanced forensic techniques. On SSDs, garbage collection complicates secure deletion.
questions.takeaway: Secure deletion = Multiple overwrites of actual data sectors; normal deletion = removes references only; SSD complicates secure deletion

questions.question: What is the DoD 5220.22-M wiping standard used in forensic drive preparation?
options: A 7-pass overwrite for government classified data
A single-pass zero-fill of all sectors
A 3-pass overwrite standard: pass 1 (zeros), pass 2 (ones), pass 3 (random data) with verification
The Department of Defense standard for classified document shredding
questions.explanation: DoD 5220.22-M (National Industrial Security Program) originally specified a 3-pass overwrite: Pass 1 = all zeros (0x00), Pass 2 = all ones (0xFF), Pass 3 = random data, followed by verification. This standard is used to sanitize forensic destination drives before use, ensuring no residual data from previous investigations contaminates new cases.
questions.takeaway: DoD 5220.22-M = 3-pass wipe: zeros → ones → random + verify; used to sanitize forensic drives before use

questions.question: What is the difference between 'data sanitization' and 'data deletion' in a forensic context?
options: Data deletion is stronger than sanitization
Sanitization is for digital evidence; deletion is for physical evidence
They are the same process
Data sanitization permanently destroys data to prevent any recovery; data deletion only removes file system references
questions.explanation: Data sanitization (secure erasure) permanently destroys data through: overwriting (software wipe), degaussing (magnetic field destroys HDD), or physical destruction (shredding). Data deletion removes references (file system entries) but leaves data recoverable. In forensics, destination drives are sanitized before use; in incident response, compromised data must be sanitized before disposal.
questions.takeaway: Sanitization = Permanent data destruction (overwrite/degauss/shred); Deletion = Reference removal (data recoverable)

questions.question: What is the role of the 'forensic examiner' vs 'forensic investigator' in a digital investigation?
options: Forensic examiner analyzes digital evidence technically; forensic investigator conducts the overall inquiry and coordinates
Forensic investigators work in courts; examiners work in labs only
Forensic examiners are law enforcement; investigators are private sector
They are the same role
questions.explanation: While sometimes used interchangeably, the forensic examiner focuses on the technical examination of digital evidence (imaging, analysis, tool operation) in a laboratory setting. The forensic investigator takes a broader role: planning the investigation, coordinating with law enforcement/legal counsel, conducting interviews, and directing the overall inquiry beyond technical analysis.
questions.takeaway: Forensic examiner = Technical analysis (lab work); Forensic investigator = Overall inquiry coordination and direction

questions.question: What does RFC 3227 provide for digital forensics?
options: Legal framework for cybercrime prosecution
Guidelines for evidence collection and archiving, including the order of volatility and principles for evidence handling
Technical specifications for forensic image formats
Standards for forensic laboratory accreditation
questions.explanation: RFC 3227 (Guidelines for Evidence Collection and Archiving) provides best practices for: order of volatility for collection, principles for forensically sound collection, evidence handling procedures, chain of custody requirements, and archiving standards. It is widely cited in digital forensic methodology documentation.
questions.takeaway: RFC 3227 = Best practices for evidence collection: order of volatility + collection principles + chain of custody guidelines

questions.question: What does the Helix Live CD provide for forensic first responders?
options: A graphical timeline analysis tool for Windows systems
A commercial forensic reporting platform
A bootable Linux environment with pre-installed forensic tools for live acquisition without touching the evidence drive
A Helix-based network monitoring platform
questions.explanation: Helix (formerly by e-fense) was a popular bootable Linux-based forensic environment that could be booted from CD/USB on a suspect machine to perform live acquisition and analysis without writing to the evidence drive. It included tools for memory acquisition, disk imaging, and preliminary analysis while maintaining evidence integrity.
questions.takeaway: Helix = Bootable forensic Linux CD/USB; live acquisition on suspect machine without evidence drive modification

questions.question: What is the SIFT Workstation (SANS Investigative Forensic Toolkit)?
options: A mobile forensics platform for Android analysis
A commercial forensic hardware device
A Windows-based forensic imaging software suite
